Name: 21_waste_collection_solid_works
Representative_Docs: ['Please note that trash collection will be delayed or operating with reduced services as the Carrboro Public Works Department copes with damages to its public facility and fleet. Public Works lost five of seven of its solid waste trucks, approximately 90% of equipment and 60% of passenger vehicle fleet.', 'ℹ️ Solid Waste Collection Carrboro Public Works crews will continue collecting waste throughout the week. One side loader is currently picking up debris, and a second side loader borrowed from Orange County is expected to be in service soon. Regarding dumpster service, one truck borrowed from Chapel Hill is in service collecting dumpsters at commercial properties and apartment complexes. Bulk waste is also being serviced, thanks to a boom truck borrowed from High Point. All efforts are concentrated on areas hardest hit by flood damage. We thank you for your patience.', 'Your patience is appreciated. The fleet shop crew was able to get one side loader back in service and that truck will begin collection of residential solid waste today, beginning with the regular Monday collection route. \xa0 The staff continues to locate resources to fill other solid waste truck fleet needs.']

It will use 20 acres that the town owns by the North Carolina Railroad corridor. See the Future Train Station project page. Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation —\xa0Sewer lines will be repaired and relined in the Lawndale Basin in northern Hillsborough. See the Lawndale Basin Sewer Rehabilitation project page. Parking Study —\xa0The town is comprehensively studying its parking supply and the demand for parking in the downtown area and West Hillsborough to address existing conditions and future needs. See the Parking Study page. Highway 86 North Facility — The facility will be renovated and storage outbuildings will be constructed to allow use by multiple town operations. See the Highway 86 North Facility project page. Ridgewalk Greenway —\xa0A feasibility study has been conducted on a future north-south greenway that would provide a bike/pedestrian route from the future train station to the downtown. See the Ridgewalk Greenway project page. River Pumping Station Relocation — The town is working to replace the 1970s-era River Pumping Station, moving it from the Eno River floodplain and increasing sewer capacity in the area. See the River Pumping Station Relocation project page. South Churton Street Multimodal Corridor Study —\xa0South Churton Street is being studied to develop design concepts that contribute to the town's character and meet the goals of a state-funded project to widen the road from Interstate 40 to the Eno River. The town does list large private development occurring or under review within town limits on the Development Projects page.", "Water and sewer rates fund Hillsborough's water and sewer fund. This enterprise fund pays for providing water and sewer service, including operating, maintaining and improving the water and sewer system. The town's water and sewer rates are reviewed annually as part of the budget process. The rates schedule that is adopted with the budget becomes effective July 1 with the new fiscal year. West Fork Eno Reservoir (Photo by Schnabel Engineering) Board of Commissioners Budget and Finances Financial Services Utilities Department Current Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $12.39 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $24.78 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $17.44 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $34.88 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $24.16 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $48.32 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $34.01 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $68.02 for up to 2,000 gallons Proposed Rates In-Town Customers Water Rate $13.32 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $26.64 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $18.75 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $37.50 for up to 2,000 gallons Out-of-Town Customers Water Rate $25.97 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Water Fee $51.94 for up to 2,000 gallons Sewer Rate $36.56 per 1,000 gallons Minimum Sewer Fee $73.12 for up to 2,000 gallons Each month, water and sewer customers who use the minimum amount charged or less spend a combined: 2.98 cents per gallon in town. 5.82 cents per gallon out of town. Residents used an average of about 3,440 gallons of water and sewer monthly in the last fiscal year —\xa0July 2024 through June 2025. Fees and Charges Schedule About the Rates Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated. Comparing Water and Sewer Rates Comparing rates is not simple, as the cost of service for each water and sewer system is based on a number of diverse factors. Customers should not expect a gallon of water to cost the same across water systems, notes an Environmental Finance Blog article . Annual Rates Research Annual rates research is conducted by the Environmental Finance Center at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. See: North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Dashboard North Carolina Water and Wastewater Rates Report Characteristics for Hillsborough Characteristics for Hillsborough that affect rates include that it: Is in the Central Pines Council of Government region. Is within the Upper Neuse River watershed and\xa0the Falls Lake watershed. Is affected by wastewater treatment mandates under the Falls Lake Rules, which are state mandates to protect and restore the water quality of Falls Lake. Charges for a minimum usage each billing cycle. Additional use is billed per 1,000 gallons. Charges out-of-town customers a rate that is 1.95 times the rate for in-town customers. Serves about 6,910 accounts and an estimated 13,995 people as of the start of Fiscal Year 2026. Had average residential customer use of about 3,440 gallons of water per month in the last fiscal year — July 2024 through June 2025. Staff Title Phone Email Bradford, Emily Budget Director 919-296-9427 Email"]

Name: 0_water_chlorine_grease_fats
Representative_Docs: ["Annual Chlorine Disinfection For one month each year, the town is required to disinfect drinking water with chlorine. The state requires this annual change in utilities that normally use chloramines,\xa0a compound of chlorine and\xa0ammonia. Chlorine is a more intense disinfectant,\xa0and\xa0the annual change helps ensure\xa0a high level of disinfection in water mains. Other utilities in the region conduct the disinfection at the same time. This includes Hillsborough’s\xa0interconnected neighboring utilities, Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the City of Durham. Effects on Customers During the annual disinfection, some customers may notice a chlorine taste or odor in Hillsborough’s drinking water. Your water remains safe to drink. To remove chlorine taste and odor from water, you can: Add a few lemon slices to a pitcher of water. The lemon’s ascorbic acid will neutralize the chlorine. Let water sit in an open container in the refrigerator or on a counter for a day or so. A wide or shallow container will allow more contact between the air and water. Filter water with activated carbon. Water pitchers with such filters are sold locally. Boil water for 15 minutes to evaporate the chlorine. If you use Hillsborough water for special purposes or for processes involving careful control of water\xa0characteristics, the town encourages you to seek advice from an appropriate technical source — such\xa0as a filter vendor or service company — about whether and how to make adjustments to your use of\xa0Hillsborough water during the month of chlorine disinfection. Annual and Periodic Water System Flushing During the annual disinfection, staff release water from fire hydrants to ensure chlorine flows through the entire system. They also perform maintenance on hydrants. The annual flushing helps remove sediment and other minerals that may have settled in the water system.\xa0This flushing can cause temporary cloudiness or discoloration of water and could discolor light-colored fabric.\xa0If discoloration or air bubbles appear in the water, customers should run cold water in a bathtub for a few minutes until the water and air bubbles clear. If the water does not clear within five minutes, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. During flushing or hydrant maintenance: Town personnel may be required to trim plantings or remove other items to ensure that adequate access to the hydrants exists for emergencies and maintenance. Community members may be asked to relocate plants and other items in street rights-of-way where necessary to provide a clear, 3-foot-wide area around a hydrant. Water Line Breaks The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment. Water Distribution Division The Water Distribution Division repairs water line breaks and maintains: 153 miles of water lines 3 water boosting pump stations 5 water tanks The division includes a backflow prevention program to prevent contaminants from entering the water supply. Awards In past years, the division has won\xa0Outstanding Water Distribution Operator of the Year from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association.", 'In the Bathroom Reduce the amount of water used: Wet your toothbrush and then turn off the faucet. Rinse your razor in a partially filled sink instead of under a running tap. Shorten your shower. Cutting out one or two minutes can save up to 700 gallons of water each\xa0month. Take a shallow bath. This saves more water than a short shower. Install low-flow showerheads or flow restrictors. Install ultra-low flush toilets and faucets if building or remodeling a home. Reduce the amount of water flushed: Flush only when necessary and put trash in the trash can. Each flush wastes 5 to 7 gallons of water, and flushed trash can clog pipes. Put a filled plastic bottle in your toilet tank. Fill the bottle with water and a\xa0small amount of pebbles to weigh it down. Place it away from operating mechanisms. This can save you 10 or more gallons of water each day. Save water for other use: Place a bucket under the bath faucet when waiting for water to warm. Use the retained cold water\xa0later for house plants or to refill the toilet tank after flushing. Use water from pet dishes or dehumidifiers to water plants. In the Kitchen and Laundry Reduce the use of running water: Keep water in the refrigerator for drinking instead of running the faucet until water gets cold. Rinse vegetables in a pan of water. Defrost frozen foods in the microwave or refrigerator. Use the garbage disposal less. Consider composting. Putting food scraps and grease and oils down kitchen sinks risks clogging sewer pipes. Fill one sink with soapy water and one with rinse water when washing dishes by hand. If you have\xa0only one sink, use a dish rack and rinse with hot water. Use the smallest amount of detergent possible when washing dishes by hand. This reduces the amount of rinse water needed and the risk of clogged pipes. Use your dishwasher and washing machine for full loads only. If considering the replacement of large appliances: Select high-efficiency washing machines. Consider that new dishwasher models are more water- and energy-efficient. Outside and Dining Out If possible for outside plantings: Reduce or eliminate watering grass, bushes and plants. Reduce or eliminate planting new plants. If possible for outside maintenance: Avoid washing vehicles at home. Most commercial car washes recycle the wash water. Soapy\xa0runoff is not good for the environment or stormwater system. Consider delaying projects that use a lot of water, such as: Carpet cleaning with your own steam cleaner. Draining and refilling a hot water heater. Filling ponds, hot tubs, fountains or pools. Masonry projects that need a water mix for grout or concrete. Painting projects that require washing brushes, rollers and trays. Power washing. When dining out, if you: Won’t be drinking water, ask that no tap water be served. Will be drinking water, ask for bottled water during water restrictions.', 'Black Residue Black residue around utility fixtures generally does not indicate a problem with water quality. It is likely due to one of the following: Bacteria feeding on oxidized iron and manganese in water. A reaction between ammonia that the town uses for disinfection and rubber in faucet rings or toilet flaps. Iron and manganese are naturally occurring minerals and are in most water across the United States. The bacteria that feed on iron and manganese are non-health threatening. As they feed on the minerals, they form red-brown or black-brown residue that can clog water systems and cause a foul odor. Hillsborough and neighboring utilities use chloramines — a compound of chlorine and ammonia — to disinfect and preserve water. Removal To remove the iron and manganese bacteria, use equal parts of white vinegar, peroxide and water to clean any residue seen. This solution can be used periodically as a preventative measure on utility fixtures. To resolve the issue of a reaction between ammonia and rubber, the rubber materials in faucets and toilets can be replaced with materials that can withstand ammonia. Check labels on replacement products. To request testing or to make utilities crews aware of the issue and location, call the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. Crews can take a water sample from the home to determine the cause and\xa0discuss solutions. Pink Residue A pink to reddish film or residue on bathroom and kitchen surfaces is likely the result of airborne bacteria in the home that produce a pinkish or dark gray film on routinely moist surfaces, such as toilet bowls, showerheads, sink drains and tiles. The bacterium is likely Serratia marcescens , which is found naturally in soil, food and animals. It produces a red pigment and thrives on moisture, dust and phosphates, such as soap and shampoo\xa0residue. The bacteria have been tied to urinary tract infections, wound infections and respiratory problems in some people. Removal To prevent the pink residue: Keep bathtubs and sinks wiped down and dry. Clean surfaces with a solution that contains chlorine. Periodically disinfect toilets with chlorine bleach. Note that abrasive cleaners may scratch fixtures and allow increased growth of bacteria. Stale Water Before resuming use of a building or during periods of underutilized use, remove stale and potentially unhealthy water in your building’s water system by\xa0flushing the water system and all water devices. For more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant. Background When buildings are vacant or operating at significantly reduced capacity for more than a few days, water sits in pipes and devices instead of flowing. The chlorine and ammonia that the\xa0Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant adds to disinfect water disappears over time.\xa0Without proper levels of disinfectant, disease-causing microorganisms can begin to grow. Corrosion control also can be affected. Flushing Water Systems and Devices Flushing in stages is best. Begin at the main service line and include all the plumbing, storage tanks, fixtures, fire suppression systems, and equipment such as water fountains and ice machines. The first flush pulls out stale water. Follow-up flushes ensure fresh water with disinfectant is drawn fully through the building. For larger buildings, a single flush isn’t enough to reestablish good water quality. The longer service is interrupted, the more flushing is needed. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers.']

Representative_Docs: ['Events No results found. The town accepts online reservation requests for park facilities and the special event banner location on Churton Street. Check If You Need a Permit Before selecting a facility, check if your event requires a permit. Special Events Permits Zoning Compliance Permits Special Events Permit Special event permits are required for public events and events that are: Over six hours long. Requesting permission for a prohibited activity, such as archery, sound amplifying equipment, and temporary chalk marking associated with sports and games on the field. See the Parks Reservations Requirements section for a list of additional prohibited activities. Applications must be received at least 60 days in advance of the requested event. Upon request, the due date for a reservation payment may be delayed until approval has been determined for a special event permit application. Zoning Compliance Permit Special event banners and signage require a\xa0zoning compliance permit\xa0from the town. In addition to the reservable banner location, up to five signs may be placed around town with the permit. Reserve a Facility Read the information on each reservation page and check sunset times before submitting a reservation. Town parks close 30 minutes after sunset. Events are limited to 75 people. The banner location is part of the “Welcome to Hillsborough” sign. Fees The fees for the special event banner are: Banner-only reservation and permit — $5. Banner reservation and up to five additional signs permit — $20. Fees and Charges Schedule Requirements Special event banners and signage require a zoning compliance permit from the town. In addition to the reservable banner location, up\xa0to five signs may be placed around town with the permit. General Information Reservation and permit applications must be submitted at least three business days before signage is placed. Town staff will remove any banners or event signs improperly located, exceeding size limits or left beyond the permitted time. Requirements for Banners The only approved location for banners is between the flagpoles that are part of the “Welcome to Hillsborough” sign at the intersection of South Churton and Mayo streets. Banners may be displayed for two weeks. They may advertise community activities, functions, festivals and similar events open to the public. Functions of a political or for-profit nature are not permitted. Banners must be: Made of a mesh material or have air vents every 3 feet. Tied to the banner supports using one-quarter-inch nylon rope at each corner. Flagpoles cannot be used. About 30 inches tall, with a maximum height of 36 inches and a maximum length of 15 feet. The banner supports are spaced about 18 feet apart. Requirements for Additional Signs No event may display more than five additional signs. Total signage is limited to 100 square feet. On Site\xa0— One sign no larger than 32 square feet may be on the lot hosting the event for the same display period. The sign may be a banner if it is securely fastened and adequately vented to pose no traffic threat. Off Site\xa0— Four signs may be displayed off site in locations noted in the permit application. They may be placed in the public right of way but not in a driveway or intersection sight triangle. The signs must be rigid (not banners), and a single side may not exceed 8 square feet. Signs may be erected 14 days before the event and must be removed within 48 hours after the event. This allows for a maximum display of 17 days. Reserve the Banner See Available Times Reserve Special Event Banner Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9470 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Reservations Administrator']

Name: 2_tax_food_prepared_beverage
Representative_Docs: ['Monthly Tax Compliance Monthly reports and tax payments for prepared food and beverage are: Due by the 25th day of the month after sales were made. Subject to penalties for nonpayment, including a possible misdemeanor charge punishable by a fine and imprisonment. Required every month from permanent mobile food vendors regardless of sales. This is in instead of paying a single-day prepaid food and beverage tax for each day of operation. Payment Methods Online with credit or debit card using the town payments and donations website. In person with cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hours are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Forms Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form (Español)', 'Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Rate The town levies a 1% sales tax on prepared foods and beverages. Proceeds fund the Tourism Board . The volunteer board\xa0reinvests the funds into: Hillsborough Visitors Center Grants, special projects and partnerships Contract services with local organizations and historic sites See Prepared Food and Beverage Tax in the Code of Ordinances. Monthly Tax Compliance Monthly reports and tax payments for prepared food and beverage are: Due by the 25th day of the month after sales were made. Subject to penalties for nonpayment, including a possible misdemeanor charge punishable by a fine and imprisonment. Required every month from permanent mobile food vendors regardless of sales. This is in instead of paying a single-day prepaid food and beverage tax for each day of operation. Payment Methods Online with credit or debit card using the town payments and donations website. In person with cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hours are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Forms Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Report Form (Español) Single-Day Tax Compliance Nonprofit organizations and merchants selling prepared food or beverages in temporary locations must submit an application and pay for a single-day prepaid food and beverage tax for each day of operation. Temporary locations include food trucks. Multiple dates may be listed on the same permit application. A separate $15 pre-paid tax must be paid for each day of operation. The tax is levied on the individual consumer (not the merchant or seller). Sales are exempt only if the end-user is tax exempt under an appropriate provision of the U.S. Internal Revenue Code. Merchants selling in temporary locations: Are subject to penalties for nonpayment of the tax, including a possible misdemeanor charge punishable by a fine and imprisonment. Are also referred to as itinerant merchants. See Prepared Food and Beverage Tax in\xa0the Code of Ordinances. Payment Methods Online using the town payments and donations website. In person with cash or check at the Financial Services Department in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hours are 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. weekdays. Forms Single-Day Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Application Single-Day Prepared Food and Beverage Tax Application (Español)', 'Mobile Food Vendor Regulations Mobile food vendors must have operating permits from the town and the Orange County Health Department . Vendors include pushcart and food truck operators. Regulations apply to those\xa0who intend to operate from a fixed location or through a route multiple days per year. See Mobile Food Vendors in the Code of Ordinances. Regular Operations within Town The town permits a specific\xa0number of food trucks within its limits for more permanent operations. Permits are issued on a first-come, first-served basis until all available permits have been issued. Apply for Permit Monthly and Single-Day Tax Compliance Mobile food vendors are required to make food and beverage tax payments: Monthly Compliance —\xa0Vendors working in a more permanent basis must file a report each month regardless of sales. Single-Day Compliance —\xa0Nonprofit organizations and merchants selling prepared food or beverages in temporary locations must submit an application and prepay for each day of operation. Name: 3_lake_carolina_falls_north
Representative_Docs: ['Falls Lake Rules Hillsborough is required to treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because it is in a watershed that drains into Falls Lake. The lake is a 12,000-acre reservoir in Durham, Granville and Wake counties. It is on the Environmental Protection Agency’s list of impaired waters and has been designated nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. To protect and restore the lake’s water quality, the state created the Falls Lake Rules to\xa0reduce the amount of nitrogen and phosphorous that enter Falls Lake through rivers and streams.\xa0The first stage of the wastewater discharge rules went into effect in 2016, and the second stage is expected in 2036. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule. Additional upgrades to the Wastewater Treatment Plant likely will be required to meet the Stage 2 wastewater rules. Read more about the rules from the Upper Neuse River Basin Association .', "Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. It also maintains pipes and infrastructure to deliver drinking water and transport wastewater for treatment. See the Water and Sewer Services page for billing, rates, service connection and other services related to the water and sewer system. West Fork Eno Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's source for water. No recreation is allowed at the reservoir. Learn More Tour our water plant See what it takes to prepare water from the Eno River for drinking. Sign Up Tour our wastewater plant What happens to the water you use? See how the town cleans the water for return to the Eno River. Sign Up Annual Reports Hillsborough's treatment plants regularly test for contaminants and prepare annual reports about their treatment processes and testing. It includes  information on our water treatment system and performance as well as water quality information from the Environmental Protection Agency. Water Quality Report Wastewater Quality Report A report is published in August each year for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It includes information on our wastewater collection and treatment systems and their performance. Wastewater Quality Report Distribution and Collection Division The Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division: Repairs water line leaks. Cleans and repairs sewer lines. Maintains water tanks, booster pump stations and sewage lift stations. Staff members are responsible for programs and projects to maintain and protect the utility system. Water Treatment Plant The water plant takes water from the Eno River, cleans and disinfects it, and then pumps it to customers.
