Corbin St. 919-296-9630 Office Hours — 9 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Mahagan, Jeff Deputy Utilities Director — Water Treatment 919-296-9671 Email Lashley, Joel Utility System Superintendent 919-296-9651 Email Strandwitz, Marie Utilities Director 919-296-9631 Email Cates, Nathan Water Treatment Plant Superintendent 919-296-9641 Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email", "Hillsborough's drinking water comes from the Eno River. Before the water is safe to drink, it must go through multiple stages of treatment, which can take several days. The Water Treatment Plant is staffed 24 hours a day, 7 days a week. Staff who maintain the pipes, pumps and tanks connected with supplying water to you must be available at all hours for emergencies. About Water Alerts Backflow Prevention Lead and Drinking Water Water Conservation Our Drinking Water Source Eno River, Lakes and Reservoir The Eno River is Hillsborough's water source. Water from the river is drawn from three sites located in the Upper Neuse River watershed. The Eno and these three sites are considered surface water\xa0— natural water that has not penetrated much below the ground. Lakes on the Eno River Lake Ben Johnston\xa0— Hillsborough takes water from this lake to make drinking water. Seven Mile Creek enters the Eno River upstream of this area. Lake Orange —\xa0The town uses this lake as a secondary source of water from the Eno. The lake is on the east fork of the river outside town limits. It is maintained by Orange County. West Fork Eno Reservoir The town owns this reservoir on the west fork of the Eno River in nearby Cedar Grove and uses it as a secondary source for water.\xa0It can store about 1.2 billion gallons of water —\xa0about two years of supply. The reservoir opened in 2000, and a second\xa0phase was completed in 2021.\xa0It includes an innovative spillway that provides more surface in a compact area for water to spill over. The dam resembles piano keys when in use. Emergency Water The town has pipe connections with neighboring utilities to receive or send water during emergencies. Receiving Water Hillsborough can receive water from Durham in an emergency. Additionally, the Hillsborough Utilities Department is working on a project that would enable the town to receive water from its southern utilities neighbor, Orange Water and Sewer Authority. Sending Water Hillsborough can send water to: City of Durham Water Management Orange-Alamance Water System, which serves some rural areas in Orange and Alamance counties Orange Water and Sewer Authority, which serves the Carrboro and Chapel Hill area History of the Reservoir In the 1980s and early 1990s, the Hillsborough community began exploring options for addressing the town’s water shortage due to a history of droughts and water restrictions and a desire to accommodate expected future growth. In 1993, Hillsborough voters approved general obligation bonds to help pay for building the reservoir’s first phase. The first phase was built with expansion in mind. The reservoir’s 20-year permit allowed for a second phase to be started by 2018. In 2000, the reservoir was made operational earlier than scheduled and before full to help provide immediate drought relief for customers under water restrictions. Severe Droughts Hillsborough experienced severe droughts in the 1920s, 1930s, 1950s, 1980s and 2000s. In 1954, schools closed at noon and the town sought federal aid because of drought conditions. That same year, drought conditions forced the town to disconnect the water of any customer found wasting it. In 1988, mandatory water restrictions were put in place. By the early 1990s, the town completed an interconnected pipeline with the City of Durham to allow regular purchases from Durham’s water system to withstand periods of droughts and water restrictions. The town could only purchase water between 10 p.m. and 6 a.m. due to demands on Durham’s system. Benefits Phase 1 of the reservoir has: Helped the town withstand several severe droughts in the 2000s. At one point, larger surrounding systems were near or below 50 days of remaining water, but Hillsborough still had enough water for 200 days. Allowed Hillsborough to accommodate new development. Many residents and businesses joining Hillsborough after 2000 would not be here today without the reservoir. The Phase 2 expansion of the reservoir: Is a long-term investment in Hillsborough’s water supply. Raised the reservoir about 10 feet, doubling its volume and providing a maximum yield of 6 million gallons of water per day. No Recreation on the Reservoir Recreation is not allowed at the West Fork Eno Reservoir or the surrounding town-owned property. This includes boating, hunting and fishing. Required Conservation Federal and state permits require a conservation easement to: Protect the town’s drinking water source. Reduce the effects of the reservoir on others. To provide\xa0a 100-foot buffer around the reservoir, the town must keep the land in a natural state with no development, recreation or access other than to maintain the reservoir. Risks The town does not have the resources to oversee public activities at the reservoir and cannot assume the risk or liability of allowing recreational activities. Recreation also could introduce invasive animals and plants. The town already funds treatment to remove a weed native to South Asia that can damage utility equipment and the environment. See the section on annual treatment for an invasive plant on this page. Seasonal Treatment for Hydrilla The state does not plan to treat the Eno River for hydrilla in 2026 due to the flooding from Tropical Storm Chantal in 2025. A survey will be conducted later this year to determine whether hydrilla treatment should resume. Typically, the state annually treats 16 miles of the Eno River with the herbicide fluridone to reduce hydrilla, an invasive aquatic plant.\xa0Applications typically start in mid-May and continue through August. The section of the river treated is\xa0below the Ben Johnston Lake dam in Orange County to Roxboro Road and U.S. 501 in Durham County. The herbicide is applied in a concentration that is safe for humans, fish and wildlife and\xa0well below limits set by the Environmental Protection Agency. Property owners next to the treated section of river should use caution in using river water for irrigation during this time. About Hydrilla Hydrilla is native to South Asia and grows rapidly, creating thick mats on the surfaces of lakes, rivers and other waterways. The weed: Negatively affects the health of fish and wildlife in the area. Can clog intakes for supplying drinking water and irrigation. Can harbor mosquitoes. Can interfere with recreation on waterways. The plant was discovered in the Eno River watershed in the early 1990s in Lake Orange, which is upstream of Hillsborough and one of the town's water sources. In 2009, biologists confirmed hydrilla in the West Fork Eno Reservoir. The town-owned reservoir is stocked with triploid grass carp to manage the invasive plant. More Information The North Carolina Division of Water Resources manages the annual treatment. Hillsborough helps fund it. For more information, contact Stormwater and Environmental Services Manager Terry Hackett by email or phone at 919-296-9621. Water Treatment Our Water Quality The town's water treatment must meet high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than 150 substances in the town's drinking water and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Water Quality Report . Water Plant Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has: A state-certified laboratory to test for bacteria daily. Two lab-certified operators who test raw and finished water at the plant every two hours\xa0— more than required by the state. Water System Testing Staff also test various points in the water system. The water system includes the pipes, pumps and tanks needed to transport and store water. Tests occur: Weekly\xa0— For bacteria at five of the farthest points in the town's water lines. Monthly — For bacteria at 15 areas. Quarterly — For disinfection byproducts. These are substances created by the process to clean water. Annually — For inorganic materials, pesticides, synthetic organic chemicals, nitrates, volatile organic compounds and secondary contaminants. Every five years — For unregulated contaminants that the Environmental Protection Agency lists for testing. Our Water Treatment Process The Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant is the processing plant for your water. The plant takes raw water from the Eno River. It then adds ingredients and applies processes to the water to make it usable. The plant focuses on: First\xa0— Cleanliness and compliance with standards. Second\xa0— Taste, odor and appearance. Water Treatment Converting raw water into drinking water includes injecting chemicals at multiple points to help remove organic\xa0material, disinfect the water and ensure longer freshness before releasing it for use by the community. The\xa0chemical process starts before the raw water reaches the Water Treatment Plant. Water released from the town's reservoir travels to Lake Ben Johnston, where the town removes water for use.\xa0At this intake location, a chemical is added to help particles in the water combine for easier removal. At the plant, more\xa0chemicals are added for the same reason and to adjust the water's color,\xa0acidity and alkalinity. Filtering Out Materials The water then goes through several filtration, aeration and clarification stages. In these stages, the water remains in large basins while being mixed at slower and slower speeds. This allows the combined large materials to drop to the bottom of the basins. Finally, the clear water\xa0moves through a fine filtration system to remove remaining particles.\xa0At this stage, the water’s turbidity is measured and filtration\xa0adjustments are made to remove any cloudiness from the water caused by large amounts of particles that\xa0are otherwise invisible to the eye. Adjusting Treatment The water is then disinfected and final adjustments to acidity and\xa0alkalinity are made before the water is pumped through pipes to homes and businesses. Throughout the\xa0treatment process, employees test the water for more than 150 contaminants, making adjustments to the\xa0treatment as needed. See: From River to Faucet: Hillsborough's Water Treatment Process video Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Request a Tour Water Distribution  and System Maintenance Annual Chlorine Disinfection For one month each year, the town is required to disinfect drinking water with chlorine. The state requires this annual change in utilities that normally use chloramines,\xa0a compound of chlorine and\xa0ammonia. Chlorine is a more intense disinfectant,\xa0and\xa0the annual change helps ensure\xa0a high level of disinfection in water mains. Other utilities in the region conduct the disinfection at the same time. This includes Hillsborough’s\xa0interconnected neighboring utilities, Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the City of Durham. Effects on Customers During the annual disinfection, some customers may notice a chlorine taste or odor in Hillsborough’s drinking water. Your water remains safe to drink. To remove chlorine taste and odor from water, you can: Add a few lemon slices to a pitcher of water. The lemon’s ascorbic acid will neutralize the chlorine. Let water sit in an open container in the refrigerator or on a counter for a day or so. A wide or shallow container will allow more contact between the air and water. Filter water with activated carbon. Water pitchers with such filters are sold locally. Boil water for 15 minutes to evaporate the chlorine. If you use Hillsborough water for special purposes or for processes involving careful control of water\xa0characteristics, the town encourages you to seek advice from an appropriate technical source — such\xa0as a filter vendor or service company — about whether and how to make adjustments to your use of\xa0Hillsborough water during the month of chlorine disinfection. Annual and Periodic Water System Flushing During the annual disinfection, staff release water from fire hydrants to ensure chlorine flows through the entire system. They also perform maintenance on hydrants. The annual flushing helps remove sediment and other minerals that may have settled in the water system.\xa0This flushing can cause temporary cloudiness or discoloration of water and could discolor light-colored fabric.\xa0If discoloration or air bubbles appear in the water, customers should run cold water in a bathtub for a few minutes until the water and air bubbles clear. If the water does not clear within five minutes, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. During flushing or hydrant maintenance: Town personnel may be required to trim plantings or remove other items to ensure that adequate access to the hydrants exists for emergencies and maintenance. Community members may be asked to relocate plants and other items in street rights-of-way where necessary to provide a clear, 3-foot-wide area around a hydrant. Water Line Breaks The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment. Water Distribution Division The Water Distribution Division repairs water line breaks and maintains: 153 miles of water lines 3 water boosting pump stations 5 water tanks The division includes a backflow prevention program to prevent contaminants from entering the water supply. Awards In past years, the division has won\xa0Outstanding Water Distribution Operator of the Year from the N.C. American Water Works Association-Water Environment Association. More Information Black Residue Black residue around utility fixtures generally does not indicate a problem with water quality. It is likely due to one of the following: Bacteria feeding on oxidized iron and manganese in water. A reaction between ammonia that the town uses for disinfection and rubber in faucet rings or toilet flaps. Iron and manganese are naturally occurring minerals and are in most water across the United States. The bacteria that feed on iron and manganese are non-health threatening. As they feed on the minerals, they form red-brown or black-brown residue that can clog water systems and cause a foul odor. Hillsborough and neighboring utilities use chloramines — a compound of chlorine and ammonia — to disinfect and preserve water. Removal To remove the iron and manganese bacteria, use equal parts of white vinegar, peroxide and water to clean any residue seen. This solution can be used periodically as a preventative measure on utility fixtures. To resolve the issue of a reaction between ammonia and rubber, the rubber materials in faucets and toilets can be replaced with materials that can withstand ammonia. Check labels on replacement products. To request testing or to make utilities crews aware of the issue and location, call the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. Crews can take a water sample from the home to determine the cause and\xa0discuss solutions. Pink Residue A pink to reddish film or residue on bathroom and kitchen surfaces is likely the result of airborne bacteria in the home that produce a pinkish or dark gray film on routinely moist surfaces, such as toilet bowls, showerheads, sink drains and tiles. The bacterium is likely Serratia marcescens , which is found naturally in soil, food and animals. It produces a red pigment and thrives on moisture, dust and phosphates, such as soap and shampoo\xa0residue. The bacteria have been tied to urinary tract infections, wound infections and respiratory problems in some people. Removal To prevent the pink residue: Keep bathtubs and sinks wiped down and dry. Clean surfaces with a solution that contains chlorine. Periodically disinfect toilets with chlorine bleach. Note that abrasive cleaners may scratch fixtures and allow increased growth of bacteria. Stale Water Before resuming use of a building or during periods of underutilized use, remove stale and potentially unhealthy water in your building’s water system by\xa0flushing the water system and all water devices. For more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant. Background When buildings are vacant or operating at significantly reduced capacity for more than a few days, water sits in pipes and devices instead of flowing. The chlorine and ammonia that the\xa0Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant adds to disinfect water disappears over time.\xa0Without proper levels of disinfectant, disease-causing microorganisms can begin to grow. Corrosion control also can be affected. Flushing Water Systems and Devices Flushing in stages is best. Begin at the main service line and include all the plumbing, storage tanks, fixtures, fire suppression systems, and equipment such as water fountains and ice machines. The first flush pulls out stale water. Follow-up flushes ensure fresh water with disinfectant is drawn fully through the building. For larger buildings, a single flush isn’t enough to reestablish good water quality. The longer service is interrupted, the more flushing is needed. Depending on the complexity of a building’s water system, facility managers should develop a comprehensive water management program for this process, but it should not replace a facility-specific Water Management Plan. Flushing Steps to Consider In addition to the following steps, consider capturing and reusing flushed water for outdoor watering or cleaning purposes. Flush all interior and exterior faucets (remove faucet aerators, if possible) for 10 to 30 minutes. Open all outlets at once to flush the service line and then open them again, individually, starting near where the water enters the building. Flush cold water first. Then flush hot water until it reaches its maximum temperature. Follow manufacturer recommendations to flush water fountains, hot water tanks, hot water recirculating loops, ice makers, dishwashers, humidifiers, and cooling towers. Water Quality Concerns For information on the quality of your water, see\xa0the annual Water Quality Report . If you have concerns about your water quality or would like more information, contact the Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The town will test water for its customers at no charge. See the section on whole house filters on this page regarding testing. Private Testing Certified Laboratories — For private water testing, find a certified lab on the North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality website by searching for drinking water laboratories. Testing Kits — If you receive any water testing kits by mail, they are from private companies unaffiliated with the town. Whole House Filters Whole house water filter systems are devices that customers can buy privately to treat their water. Issues to Keep in Mind Bacteria —\xa0Most water filters must be changed every 30 days to prevent bacteria from growing, and many water filters eliminate chlorine. The town adds chlorine to its drinking water for disinfection. Without adequate disinfection, bacteria will start to grow. Poor Water Quality —\xa0Use of a filter that eliminates chlorine will cause free ammonia to be left in the filtered water. This could cause chemical substances in the water to change, resulting in poor water quality. Specifically, ammonium salts could change to nitrites and then nitrates in a process called nitrification. Nitrate contamination can be unhealthy, particularly for infants. Hillsborough adds ammonia to water as part of its disinfection process. The ammonia bonds with chlorine to produce chloramines to cleanse water. Water Testing —\xa0If a water quality issue occurs in a home with a whole house filter system, the town cannot test the water.\xa0Whole house filters provide secondary treatment to water. Test results at homes with these filters would not provide data on the water that Hillsborough delivers. Contact Information Water Distribution Division Adron Thompson Water/Sewer Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Water Treatment Plant, 711 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-732-3621 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Cates, Nathan Water Treatment Plant Superintendent 919-296-9641 Email Lashley, Joel Utility System Superintendent 919-296-9651 Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email"]

Name: 5_backflow_prevention_device_devices
Representative_Docs: ['Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information.', 'Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information.', "The town regulates connections to its water supply to prevent contamination and to protect public safety. Backflow prevention devices ensure water flows only in one direction. A number of uses require installation of a backflow prevention device, including irrigation systems. A backflow prevention device with protection for the winter Tampering and Theft About Backflow What is it? Backflow is the unwanted reversal of the flow of water and substances.\xa0Typically, water will flow from the public water supply to the consumer’s plumbing system. When backflow occurs, the water flows from the non-drinking water source to the drinking water source and potentially contaminates it. What causes it? Backflow incidents are caused by one of the following: Back pressure — This is created when the pressure within the customer’s system becomes greater than the water supply pressure. Elevated tanks, heating systems and booster pumps are some of the main causes of back pressure. Back siphonage — This is created when the water supply main has negative or reduced pressure. Water main breaks or hydrant flushing are two of the main causes of back siphonage. About How the Town Protects Water The town has requirements to protect the public drinking water supply. Containment Protection — The town installs backflow\xa0prevention at the service meter to protect the public water supply from contamination. Isolation Protection — The town requires backflow prevention at the fixture\xa0where water is used. This protects the customer's internal drinking water plumbing from contamination. Uses Requiring Backflow Prevention Several types of facilities are required to install a backflow prevention device. If your property or business type is not included in the list below and you have a specific question about your legal requirements, contact the town's utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. Auto repair shops Auxiliary water supply (wells) Barber shops and beauty salons Breweries Buildings that reuse or recycle water Buildings with commercial or public kitchens, groundwater wells, multiple\xa0water service lines, roof tanks and elevated storage lines, or water-cooled equipment or chillers Canneries Commercial car washes Dye plants Exterminators Fire sprinkler system Food preparation facilities Food processing plants and meat or fish packers Funeral parlors Greenhouses Lawn irrigation systems Metal plating, cleaning, processing or fabricating facilities Hospitals, clinics and laboratories (including veterinary hospitals) Ice manufacturing facilities Large residential dwellings with water boilers that use rust-inhibitors or other\xa0water treatment chemicals (treated water boilers) Laundries and dry cleaners Medical and dental offices Morgues, mortuaries and autopsy facilities Nursing homes Paper processors Photo-processing facilities Printing facilities Schools and colleges Sewage treatment plants or handling facilities Slaughterhouses and live poultry processing facilities Swimming pools Supermarkets Tanneries Warehouses with toxic chemical storage Cross-Connection Control Requirements See Cross-Connection Control Policy . No Direct Connections The town prohibits direct connections between the water supply and any potential source of contamination.\xa0Potential sources of contamination must be isolated by an air gap or a reduced pressure zone backflow assembly. A direct connection would occur if a garden hose became submerged in a swimming pool, hot tub, soapy water bucket, pond or pet water dish. Water Use by Businesses Contracted businesses working in Hillsborough and needing water must do one of the following: Transport water into town. Purchase bulk water from the town. Rent a hydrant meter from the town. The orange box that is connected to some hydrants contains a meter and a backflow prevention device. See: Start or Stop Service page for bulk water purchases or hydrant meter rentals Tampering and Theft page Outdoor Faucet Requirements The town requires a hose bib vacuum breaker on every outside faucet. The vacuum breaker must be certified by the NSF 61 standard. The American Water Works Association recommends replacing vacuum breakers every five years or if they are failing. The device has failed if water is spraying out of the breaker's weep holes. Testing The town contracts with Backflow Solutions to manage its backflow test data. Customers using backflow prevention assemblies must: Have the assemblies tested when installed. Maintain their assemblies and h ave them tested annually by qualified individuals. Submit results of testing and maintenance to Backflow Solutions. The company maintains a list of licensed, registered testing agencies on its website. Contact Backflow Solutions at 800-414-4990 or bsionline@backflow.com. Visit its website at BSI Online . Process Existing Assemblies — Licensed testers must submit test results through the Backflow Solutions website . Failed Test\xa0— T he assembly must be repaired within 10 days if it fails a test. A certified tester must submit retest results on the Backflow Solutions website . New Installations — A licensed tester who performed the work must sign a printed test form. A copy must be emailed to Backflow Solutions. Preventing Backflow Backflow Prevention Methods Air Gap — An air gap is the vertical space between a water inlet and the rim of a receptacle receiving public water. Examples are a faucet or hose providing water to a bucket, sink or pool.\xa0The air gap must be at least twice the diameter of the water inlet and no smaller than 1 inch. Backflow Assembly — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet and outlet shutoff valves. It has four test cocks, making inline testing possible. Public water systems, which regulate connections to the public water supply, generally require backflow assemblies rather than devices. Backflow Device — This mechanical unit prevents backflow. It does not have inlet or outlet shutoff valves and usually does not have test cocks. Inline testing is not possible for devices without test cocks. The devices are not approved by the University of Southern California Foundation for Cross-Connection Control and Hydraulic Research. Backflow devices generally are governed by plumbing code when a building is constructed or upfitted. Elimination —\xa0Eliminating the cross-connection is necessary when the hazard has been improperly installed and plumbed or when the hazard is no longer in use. Common Backflow Prevention Assemblies and Uses Double Check Valve Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler system with no booster pumps, most commercial establishments Double Check Valve Detector Assembly Non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as non-health hazards Reduced Pressure Detector Assembly Health and non-health hazards Back pressure/back siphonage Fire sprinkler systems that are classified as health hazards Reduced Pressure Principal Assembly (preferred assembly) Health and non-health hazards Hospitals, car washes, lawn irrigation, mortuaries Common Backflow Prevention Devices and Uses Pressure Vacuum Breaker Back siphonage only Multi-zone irrigation systems Hose Connection Vacuum Breaker (not testable) Back siphonage only Garden hose connections Protecting Devices in Winter Protection All backflow devices are required to be in a class 1 or 2 enclosure that meets the American Society of Sanitary Engineering’s 1060 standard. These enclosures help protect against frost and freezing. Steps to protect backflow devices in winter include: Adding pipe insulation, an insulated cover or a heat lamp and heat trace tape. The heat tape should be warm when touched if working properly. Wrapping the protective cover of the device with a blanket or other type of insulator. Dripping water from an interior faucet to have continuous movement of water through the assembly. Backflow devices for irrigation can be removed for winterization. They must be retested when reinstalled in the spring. Repair or Replacement For repair or replacement of a device that has frozen and burst, contact a business certified to repair or replace backflow preventers and then have the device tested. For a list of licensed, registered testing agencies, visit the Backflow Solutions website at BSI Online . If the preventer is replaced, contact Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor Troy Miller to update your account information. Recommendations for Pool and Hot Tub Owners Due to the risk for backflow, the town recommends that customers filling swimming pools, hot tubs, ponds or other bodies of water: Use the air gap method. See the section on backflow prevention methods. Avoid times when hydrants are opened for flushing. This occurs in March and occasionally in the fall. Water System Flushing Lower Water Pressure —\xa0When hydrants are flushed, pressure in the water system drops. Water could backflow into the public water supply system if water main pressure is lower and a hose becomes submerged in a body of water being filled. Chemical Change —\xa0During the annual March flushing, the town’s water is disinfected by chlorination instead of chloramines (ammonia and chlorine). The state requires this change in disinfection for one month each year. The change could upset the balance of a pool, pond or spa. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Water/Sewer Facility,\xa0715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Contact Utilities Staff Title Phone Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email"]

Name: 6_parks_park_riverwalk_greenways
Representative_Docs: ['Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence.', "Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans.", "Hillsborough offers many opportunities for recreation. It does not provide recreational programming. Town-maintained parks and greenways are open daily from 7 a.m. until 30 minutes after sunset. The Public Space and Sustainability Division: Manages town parks and greenways. Implements decisions of the Parks and Recreation Board . Gold Park includes swings and other play equipment. Parks and Recreation Board Proposed Greenway Reservations Riverwalk Greenway Town Map Town Parks and Greenways See the Town Map for directions and amenities. Greenways Parks Calvin Street Greenway — This short greenway\xa0allows pedestrian and bicycle access from downtown and adjacent neighborhoods to Gold Park and Riverwalk. Riverwalk Greenway —\xa0This greenway along the Eno River connects Occoneechee Mountain State Natural Area and trails east of town. See the Riverwalk page. Community Parks Cates Creek Park —\xa01445 Cates Creek Parkway. This community park has a pollinator garden and a skate spot . The multi-use field with soccer goals can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. Gold Park —\xa0415 Dimmocks Mill Road. This community park includes a dog park and a part of the Riverwalk greenway. The multi-use field and large picnic shelter can be reserved. See the Reservations and Bee City USA pages. See the park's trail map . Natural Area Kings Highway Park —\xa01001 Ben Johnston Road. This natural area with trails is surrounded in part by Lake Ben Johnston. It includes a kayak launch . Neighborhood and Pocket Parks Hillsborough Heights Park —\xa0510 Terrell Road Mayo Park — South side of West King Street Murray Street Park —\xa0225 Murray St. Turnip Patch Park —\xa0333 W. King St. Dog Park The dog park is a fenced-in area in Gold Park where dogs area allowed to be off leash. It has the same hours as Gold Park. Dog Park Rules Dogs must be: At least 6 months old and not in heat. Current on all required vaccinations, with rabies tags displayed. On a maximum 6-foot leash before entering and when leaving the facility. Within sight and under voice control of owner at all times. Immediately removed if showing aggression. Owners must: Collect and dispose of all dog waste. Help keep the off-leash area safe for all visitors. Report all dog bites to Orange County Animal Control. Call 919-942-7387 or 911. Ground Covering The dog park's ground is covered with shredded hardwood mulch that: Is safe for use by children and dogs. Helps reduce dust, dirt and the ground cover’s movement. Grass has not been planted because maintenance would be difficult with the park's heavy use and because establishing grass would require several seasons of nonuse. Self-Guided Walks Interpretive Signage The town's interpretive signage helps provide context to sites within Hillsborough. The signs provide understanding of the town's history, environment and culture. Most of the signs are along the Riverwalk greenway, but there are some at other key points of interest in town. See Signage Map Treasure Trees The Treasure Trees Program recognizes the preservation of significant trees in the Hillsborough area. Trees are selected by the Tree Board. View them on the Treasure Trees page. About Our Recreation No recreation is allowed at the town's reservoir. See the About Water page. Building in Flood-Prone Areas Undeveloped floodplains often serve the need for public parks and recreation. The Riverwalk greenway and Gold Park were built in their locations to provide recreation in areas that otherwise would not be developed. These recreation facilities require cleanup after a severe weather event to remove hazards. They are open for use many more days throughout the year than they are closed for weather and maintenance. Built to Withstand Flooding Riverwalk is located partially within Gold Park and was built along a sewer easement by the Eno River. Both recreation facilities were designed to withstand flooding. Gold Park’s dog park was built with a chain-link fence to allow floodwaters through. When flooding is expected, the fencing is left open to allow water carrying debris to flow through without damaging the fence. Severe Weather Parks and greenways are closed at times for the safety of the community and emergency workers. Hazards can exist even after a storm with high winds or snow has passed or after floodwaters have receded. Parks and greenways: Will remain closed following severe weather until the town can inspect the areas and remove any hazards. Are not a priority for snow and ice removal. They will be closed in deteriorating wintry conditions until staff determines they are safe. During Closures Heed any closure signs or barriers for parks and trails. Those who choose to use the town’s parks and greenways do so at their own risk. By entering closed areas and by moving or removing closure signs and barriers, you also: Slow cleanup efforts and delay the reopening of parks and greenways for everyone. Force public works and public safety staff away from other duties to reinstall closure signs and barriers. Force emergency workers away from other duties for potential rescues. Risk the safety of others. Subscribe to town news and follow @HillsboroughGov social media to stay informed\xa0about closures and reopenings. Shade at Parks Town staff and appointed boards are working to provide shade options at parks. Fast-growing shade trees have been planted around the perimeter of Cates Creek and Gold parks. Trees are planted in available spaces around the play areas but not inside due to spacing regulations for the play equipment. Because of the play equipment's current height and spacing regulations, sunshades in the play areas would be installed at a height that would provide little sun blockage during the day and that would make the shades a safety risk in high winds. Water Fountains Water is turned off at exterior drinking fountains at parks when the first freezing temperatures of winter are forecasted. Water service then is restored in the spring when the danger of freezing temperatures has passed. This helps avoid water line breaks, which can be costly to repair and disruptive to the use of recreational areas. Make a Recommendation The Tree Board makes planting decisions for town spaces. Plants typically are added between November and March to improve survival due to less heat and more precipitation. The Parks and Recreation Board advises the town on recreation improvements and implements recommendations in recreation master plans. Other Recreation Recreation Offerings from Others Exchange Club Park is owned and maintained by the Exchange Club. Contact the club at 919-732-9283 for more information. Orange County provides recreational programming, a senior center and additional parks in and around town, including Fairview and River parks. See the county's: Trails and greenways map Parks locator map For other recreation options in the area and maps, visit the Hillsborough Visitors Center . Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9600 Office Hours —\xa08 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Public Space and Sustainability Staff Title Phone Email Trueblood, Stephanie Public Space and Sustainability Manager 919-296-9481 Email Rhew, Lindsay Public Works and Public Space Administrative Support Specialist 919-296-9600 Email"]

Name: 7_community_course_help_town
Representative_Docs: ["The program is intended to: Increase community members' knowledge, interest and ability to influence and participate in town government. Reduce barriers to participating in and influencing town government decisions. This occurs through increased understanding of key processes and operations of the town. Assist in building community members' interest in serving the community through future service. Learn from community members attending the course.", "Course Basics The multiweek course is coordinated by the Communications Division and includes time with the Administrative Services, Community Services, Financial Services, Police and Utilities departments and  staff from the following divisions and operations: Budget Fleet Maintenance Planning and Economic Development Public Space and Sustainability Public Works Stormwater and Environmental Services Tours Visits are made to: A fire station in Hillsborough to learn about the Orange Rural Fire Department and the Hillsborough Police Department. The town contracts with Orange Rural for fire service. The town's water and sewer facilities, including the water and wastewater treatment plants. Program Goals The program is intended to: Increase community members' knowledge, interest and ability to influence and participate in town government. Reduce barriers to participating in and influencing town government decisions. This occurs through increased understanding of key processes and operations of the town. Assist in building community members' interest in serving the community through future service. Learn from community members attending the course.", "New to Town? See information on this page\xa0to help you quickly get settled and feeling at home in our small town. Want to Get More Involved? See information on this page to help you learn more about the town. Volunteering for pollinator garden workdays is a great way to meet other Hillsborough residents. Stormwater Fees Tax Rates Water and Sewer Rates Economic Development Around Town Arts and Events Hillsborough is home to a thriving arts and culture scene, as well as many events. View galleries, shops and upcoming events on the Hillsborough Visitors Center website. Development Private Development Private development is a project not undertaken by the town or any other government. The town does list large private development occurring or under review within town limits on the Development Projects page. Public Projects See what the town is developing on the Public Projects page. History Hillsborough is home to a thriving arts and culture scene, as well as many events. View galleries, shops and upcoming events on the Hillsborough Visitors Center website. Parks and Greenways Hillsborough has green spaces nestled within its trees, including the Riverwalk greenway, which follows the Eno River through downtown. See the town’s parks and greenways on the Recreation Facilities page. View recreation facilities available across the county on the Orange County website. Be Informed Emergency Alerts The town issues emergency notifications through OC Alerts , Orange County's mass notification system. The system is a partnership of Orange Water and Sewer Authority and the governments in Orange County. OWASA provides water service to the Carrboro and Chapel Hill community. See the Ready Hillsborough page for emergency resources. Town News The town provides regular updates through news releases, social media and a monthly newsletter . A weekly digest of town news releases and key reminders are emailed on Fridays. Time-sensitive emails are sent as needed. The newsletter is inserted into paper water and sewer bills and included in the weekly digest at the start of each month. Follow @HillsboroughGov on Facebook , Instagram and X . Follow Town of Hillsborough on Nextdoor . Subscribe to town emails. Get Involved Board Meetings Meetings of the elected and appointed boards are open to the public. They typically are in the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Hillsborough Board of Commissioners — Meets the second and fourth Monday of each month, typically taking a break in July. Appointed Boards — See their individual\xa0pages for meeting schedules. Subscribe to meeting notices and see the agenda and minutes site for meeting information and materials. Civic Engagement Course The town offers a course over several weeks aimed at helping community members increase their knowledge of town government, as well as their interest and ability in influencing and participating in town decisions. See the Government 101 page. Engage Hillsborough The town offers periodic events for community members and staff to connect and discuss a topic. The town also seeks input at times through surveys. Check the Engage Hillsborough page for current or upcoming ways to engage. Volunteer Opportunities The town offers multiple ways to volunteer or help others in the community. Consider: Serving on appointed boards . Serving as a community liaison to help share information from the town and let staff know of concerns and questions in the community. Contact Communications . Improving the town’s public spaces\xa0by joining a team to remove invasive plants or attending various workdays, including to remove trash from roadsides and help with pollinator gardens. Contact Public Space . Donating to the Water Assistance Program to help neighbors pay their water bills. See a Quick Guide to Services Public Safety Emergency workers are dispatched through 911. Fire — Orange Rural Fire Department provides fire service to Hillsborough through a contract. Law Enforcement — Hillsborough Police Department provides police services within town limits. Orange County Sheriff’s Office provides services in unincorporated Orange County. Property improvements or uses Contact the Town Contact the Hillsborough Planning and Economic Development Division using the contact form or by calling 919-732-1270 before signing contracts or purchasing supplies for: Adding a deck or room or enclosing an open space. Making yard improvements such as outbuildings, fences and patios. Adding accessory dwelling units. Running a business from your home. Contact the County Call the Orange County Building Inspections Division at 919-245-2575 before signing contracts or purchasing supplies for: Renovating your home. Changing or improving electrical, heating, air conditioning or plumbing systems in your home. Adding any systems to an outbuilding. Street Maintenance The Hillsborough Public Works Division oversees maintenance of town-owned streets. Some roads in town are private or maintained by the state. See the Street Maintenance page to learn more. Solid Waste Bulk Items The town collects bulk items, such as large appliances, monthly on the second full week of the month, with the week starting on Sunday. See the Garbage Collection page for items that can be collected. Garbage The Public Works\xa0Division provides residential curbside garbage collection services within town limits as a service paid for by property taxes.\xa0To start residential service, call 919-296-9600 or use the Contact Public Works form. Multi-family dwellings receive commercial services through independent vendors. For routes and services, see the Curbside Collections Calendar . For information on special collections and requirements, see the Garbage Collection page. Rollout Carts Video Recycling Orange County provides recycling collection services on Wednesdays in Hillsborough.\xa0To set up new recycling service: Use the Orange County recycling contact form . Email recycling@orangecountync.gov . Call 919-968-2788. Rollout Carts Video Yard Waste Yard waste is collected weekly. Leaves can be placed in compostable bags year-round with a limit of 10 bags per household each week. Loose leaves are collected\xa0November through January. During the seasonal loose leaves collection, the town offers delivery of bulk leaves for compost or mulch. See the Yard Waste Collection page for requirements and additional information. Water and Sewer Accounts and Billing Hillsborough provides water and sewer services within town limits and to some properties outside town limits. Connection —\xa0Service should be requested at least three business days in advance. See the Start or Stop Service page. Electronic Bills and Online Payment —\xa0Water and sewer customers are encouraged to sign up for electronic bills and to pay online through the payments and donations site . Leaks —\xa0Customers may request bill adjustments if a water line break has resulted in extraordinary charges for a given billing period.\xa0Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a12-month period. See the Water and Sewer Billing and Adjustments page. Rates —\xa0To view and understand the town's water and sewer rates, see the Water and Sewer Rates page. Water and Sewer Services Issues Emergencies —\xa0Report water and sewer emergencies immediately to the Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant at 919-732-3621. The plant is staffed 24 hours. Leave a message. Sewer Cleanout Pipes and Rings — Report broken sewer cleanout pipes and rings to utilities staff. The broken pipe should be repaired quickly to prevent stormwater from entering the sewer system. The pipe's protective ring must be kept in place. The capped pipes are located on the edge of rights of way and provide easy access to a property's main sewer line. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9651. Water Tampering and Theft —\xa0If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the Hillsborough Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division at 919-296-9651. See the Tampering and Theft page. Water and Sewer Responsibilities Avoid Blocked Pipes Fats, Oils and Grease — Keep fats, oils and grease out of drains. They will stick to plumbing and sewer lines and eventually cause sewer backups or overflows. Prevent this by: Discarding used fats, oils and grease with other garbage. Minimizing use of garbage disposals. Scraping plates into trash cans. Using paper towels to wipe leftover oils and fats from pots and pans. The town provides fat trappers to water and sewer customers at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9653. Wipes — Flush the 3 P's only (pee, poo and toilet paper). Anything else can lead to overflows. Wipes labeled as flushable are not flushable and will clog the sewer system. No Wipes in the Pipes Video Be Aware of Easements and Town Assets Easements —\xa0Water and sewer easements give the Utilities Department the right to access, maintain, repair or replace a town asset, such as a water or sewer main. The town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds, fountains, patios, decks or other structures that impede access to the easement and maintenance of the infrastructure. Fences over easements require an encroachment agreement and other permissions. Contact Utilities or call 919-296-9630 before installing a fence. Private Service Lines —\xa0Homeowners are responsible for these private service lines: The water line between the meter and the home The sewer line between the cleanout pipe and the home. The cleanout pipe's protective ring must be kept in place. The capped pipe provides easy access to your home's main sewer line. Utility Lines and Devices —\xa0Know where utility lines are before digging, and leave access around fire hydrants and other utility devices. Call 811 to have public lines marked for free. Private service lines require marking from private companies. See the North Carolina 811 website. Follow Irrigation Rules Backflow Preventer — Customers with pools or separate irrigation systems must have a backflow preventer. See Backflow Prevention . Spray Irrigation — Spray irrigation requires a separate meter and has limited days and hours for use. Systems must have moisture sensors and should be checked annually for leaks. See: Irrigation Systems on the Water Conservation page Start or Stop Service page"]

Name: 8_usage_water_minimum_use
Representative_Docs: ["Stage 3: Rationing This stage begins when the town has less than 90 days of supply. All customers must reduce water use by 30%, except for special medical use. Customers are not required to use less than the minimum usage billed. No outdoor water usage is allowed, other than for emergency use or fire protection. Irrigation water meters will be turned off or removed by the town. The town will: Lower water pressure in its distribution system by reducing tank storage levels to the extent possible. Capture any water flushed from water lines for water quality concerns and return it to the Eno River upstream of the town's raw water intake. Use other water sources if feasible, investigating such sources as rock quarries, ponds, springs, and other utility systems. Enhance public awareness of the daily water usage, reservoir level and goal for water usage.", "Policy The mayor can issue a proclamation to restrict water use when water is in short supply in the Eno River, Lake Orange or the town's reservoir. The level of restrictions is determined by the available water supply. Construction-related activities that are not essential and that require water should not be undertaken during a declared water shortage. See the full drought policy . Stage 1: Voluntary Restrictions This stage begins when the town has less than 180 days of supply. Customers are encouraged in this stage to reduce water use by 10%.\xa0The town also may: Offer\xa0water-saving devices. Provide daily or weekly reservoir level and water usage statistics. Recommend water usage reduction techniques. Stage 2: Mandatory Restrictions This stage begins when the town has less than 135 days of supply. Customers must reduce water use by 10% and are encouraged to reduce use by 20%. No pressure washing or private car washing is allowed. Outdoor water use is limited to two days per week. Properties with odd-numbered street addresses may water on Tuesday and Saturday. Even-numbered properties may water on Wednesday and Sunday. The town must: Offer\xa0water-saving devices. Provide daily or weekly reservoir level and water usage statistics. Recommend water usage reduction techniques. Stage 3: Rationing This stage begins when the town has less than 90 days of supply. All customers must reduce water use by 30%, except for special medical use. Customers are not required to use less than the minimum usage billed. No outdoor water usage is allowed, other than for emergency use or fire protection. Irrigation water meters will be turned off or removed by the town. The town will: Lower water pressure in its distribution system by reducing tank storage levels to the extent possible. Capture any water flushed from water lines for water quality concerns and return it to the Eno River upstream of the town's raw water intake. Use other water sources if feasible, investigating such sources as rock quarries, ponds, springs, and other utility systems. Enhance public awareness of the daily water usage, reservoir level and goal for water usage.", "Minimum Usage Charge The town charges for a minimum amount of water and sewer use each month. Additional use for both is billed per 1,000 gallons. Providing the availability of water and sewer service costs the town money even if a customer uses no water that month. The costs include: Managing and protecting Hillsborough's water source. Treating, storing and distributing drinking water. Collecting, treating and returning used water to the environment. Managing water and sewer billing, including meter reading, billing software, and bill printing and mailing. These operations also have costs for qualified workers, facilities, vehicles, equipment, chemicals, electricity, gas, compliance with regulations, and administrative needs. Minimum Fee Versus Base Fee All water and sewer systems charge a base fee or a minimum usage fee to ensure they can provide service. Base fee — No water is provided  with the monthly charge. Minimum usage fee — Water is provided with the monthly charge up to the minimum number of gallons set. In-Town Versus Out-of-Town Rates The most recent published annual research in North Carolina shows a higher outside rate is charged by 98% of water systems and 95% of sewer systems that offer service outside their geographical limits. The annual study is provided by the UNC School of Government's Environmental Finance Center and the North Carolina League of Municipalities. Out-of-town water and sewer fees are higher than in-town fees due to: Cost of Service — The operations, maintenance, pumping of water and sewer, and reading of meters can be more costly in areas outside of town, mainly because of greater distance and travel time from the town’s water or wastewater treatment plants. Fewer customers in the area to spread the cost can make the service more expensive. Growth Management — P roviding water and sewer service to developments outside of town can cause the town to lose some ability to attract development that would improve its tax base, help improve the economies of scale in providing services, and potentially ease the tax burden on residents.  Lower in-town fees also can make annexation more desirable in areas that have water and sewer service. Investment Risk — In-town customers bear more of the investment risks of owning and operating the water and sewer system. When revenue bonds are issued to finance large projects, like the reservoir, the town pledges its full  rate setting authority for water and sewer as collateral to ensure the debt will be repaid. Equity — In-town customers bear more of the burden of financing and facilitating the water and sewer system’s operations. In addition to water and sewer payments, their municipal property taxes help pay for positions and resources that support the water and sewer operations. They also subsidize the use of Hillsborough services by out-of-town customers who live near the town’s corporate limits, including: Streets Police protection when inside town limits Recreation facilities, such as Gold Park, Kings Highway Park and the Riverwalk greenway Understanding the Cost of Providing Service Every community has different conditions that affect the ability to provide safe and reliable water and sewer service. Below are primary reasons that Hillsborough’s water and sewer rates are higher than those of some surrounding communities. Small System —\xa0Hillsborough operates a small water and sewer system. A larger system can spread the costs of operations among more customers. Small Water Supply —\xa0The Eno River does not provide a large volume of water. The town is  near the river's beginning, and the drainage basin that feeds into Hillsborough’s waterways is relatively small. Partnering with a neighboring system is likely not feasible. The Carrboro-Chapel Hill area\xa0is in a different river basin, and a past evaluation found\xa0costs for partnering with Durham to be too expensive. The town built a reservoir in the late 1990s and completed an expansion of it in 2021 to address water capacity needs because of expected growth and a history of droughts. Debt Management —\xa0A portion of  water and sewer bill payments helps pay the debt on projects to provide clean and reliable water and sewer service, including: Wastewater Treatment Plant upgrades —\xa0$17.8 million financed with payments until 2034. Reservoir expansion —\xa0$10.2 million financed with payments until 2042. Roads project for reservoir expansion —\xa0$3.3 million financed with payments until 2030. By law, the town must pass a balanced budget each year. Planned expenses cannot fall below expected revenue, and debt payments cannot create a deficit. State Rules —\xa0Hillsborough must treat its wastewater at higher and more expensive standards than other sewer systems because its watershed drains into Falls Lake. The lake has been deemed impaired by the Environmental Protection Agency and nutrient-sensitive by the North Carolina Division of Water Resources. The state's Falls Lake Rules to protect and restore the lake’s water quality\xa0are said to be the nation’s strictest limits on treated wastewater. In 2014, Hillsborough completed upgrades to its wastewater treatment plant in part to meet the Stage 1 wastewater rule that went into effect in 2016. A large portion of customers' sewer payments helps pay debt on the plant upgrades. Stage 2 rules, expected in 2036,\xa0likely will require more upgrades. Expensive and Older Infrastructure —\xa0Hillsborough has more sewage pump stations than a typical municipality because of its terrain and likely because of low upfront costs.\xa0Gravity is the most cost-effective way to transport water and wastewater. Pump stations are expensive to operate, require a large amount of staff time and can result in overflows and state fines when they fail.\xa0The town operates 25 stations in about 5 square miles. Most were built by developers, are old and were not built to today's standards. The town also must budget to replace other infrastructure. Some were installed well before the town's treatment plants were built. Maintenance Commitment —\xa0Water and sewer services are expensive to provide. The United States  poorly maintains its drinking and wastewater infrastructure, earning grades of C- and D+ in the 2025 Report Card for America's Infrastructure from the American Society of Civil Engineers. Across North Carolina , $20 billion is needed to maintain and improve drinking water infrastructure and $12.6 billion is needed for wastewater infrastructure. Hillsborough is committed to taking care of its operations. Your water and sewer bill reflects your contribution to the labor and the physical infrastructure required to have clean water available and to have wastewater removed and safely returned to the environment. The town does not profit from providing water and sewer service. It uses water and sewer revenue only for the operation, maintenance and improvement of the town’s water and sewer system. Videos Full Cost Pricing of Water Water: What You Pay For Understanding Enterprise Funds The Water and Sewer Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Stormwater Utility Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes.\xa0No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Water and Sewer Fund pays for providing water and sewer service. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from water and sewer charges. Efforts to Provide Water and Sewer Rates Relief The town is committed to finding ways to help relieve customers from the high cost of funding quality, reliable water and sewer service. Efforts include: Rates Study — State law prevents the town from charging different rates to customers based on income. A rate study to evaluate the town’s rate structures and to begin exploring other options to lower costs for customers was completed in Fiscal Year 2024. This study weighed the expected costs for operations and large projects against required revenue between fiscal years 2025 and 2040. It showed the need for increases in rates in Fiscal Year 2025 and in future years. The model helps the town plan for meeting needs and important financial metrics, like days of cash on hand and debt coverage ratio. Lower Minimum Usage — Since 2013, t he town has reduced the monthly minimum number of gallons that customers are charged for water and sewer use from 3,000 to 2,000 gallons. Spreading the reduction over a number of years minimized the effect  of losing revenue. Water Assistance Program — The town started the Water Assistance Program in 2006 to help eligible customers pay water and sewer bills. Candidates are recommended by the Orange County Department of Social Services or a local church. Innovation — Town employees regularly increase efficiency of the water and sewer system through innovation, defer lesser priority projects, and perform much work in house. The United States Environmental Protection Agency has included the town’s wastewater treatment plant in a case study on innovation efforts for the treatment of wastewater. Pump Station Reduction — The town is working to reduce the number of pump stations it operates. The pumps keep sewage flowing in low areas, but they are expensive to maintain and the town has more than a typical municipality. Since 2005, two pump stations have been eliminated."]

Name: 11_leaves_bags_brush_collection
Representative_Docs: ['Brush The town collects woody material from trees and shrubs weekly as part of its brush collection. Most homes receive collection on their regular garbage collection days. Homes on the\xa0following streets receive collection on Wednesday: Lydia and East Margaret lanes Streets including Caine, North and South Cameron, East Corbin, Huddle, East King, Mitchell, East Orange, East Queen, East Tryon, and East Union. Requirements Keep trash and loose leaves out of brush. Ensure brush is no larger than 6 feet in length and 6 inches in diameter. Place brush within 4 feet of the curb, 3 feet away from obstructions and not below trees or power lines. Stack limbs in one pile, with all limbs in the same direction. Place small amounts of brush in biodegradable paper bags. Or tie small amounts into bundles weighing less than 30 pounds. Use jute or sisal twine for the bundles. Do not use plastic or cotton twine. Allow a bagful or several armfuls of sticks to accumulate before placing at the curb. Christmas trees can be collected with brush with restrictions. See the Additional Information section.', 'Bagged Leaves and Grass The town collects leaves and grass clippings year-round in paper or biodegradable bags. Collection occurs on brush collection days. Name: 12_budget_fiscal_year_plan
Representative_Docs: ["Budget Budget The budget is a financial plan for the fiscal year (July 1-June 30).\xa0Hillsborough uses a three‐year format to better see any fiscal or operational challenges.  The budget's first year is legally binding. Years 2 and 3 show needs to plan for. The budget contains: An estimate of proposed expenditures. The proposed means of financing the expenditures. Goals and objectives of the expenditures. The Budget Division is responsible for preparing the town's budget.\xa0When possible, the town applies for federal, state and other grants to cover some of the cost of town projects. Two volunteer boards also have annual budgets to handle funds they oversee: the Tourism Board and Tourism Development Authority . November — Town staff begin working on budget proposals for the next fiscal year, which starts July 1. January-March — The mayor and commissioners meet with key staff members in a retreat during the winter to plan for the budget. The retreat gives staff guidance on the priorities and direction for the budget. May — The town manager presents a recommended budget to the town board. The community is invited to review and share comments on the budget at a public hearing later in the month. The board also conducts a work session to discuss the budget at the same meeting. June —\xa0A second work session occurs in June if needed. The board votes on the budget at a regularly scheduled meeting in June. July — The approved budget goes into effect on July 1. Funds The town’s three budgetary funds are the General Fund, Stormwater Fund, and Water and Sewer Fund. General Fund — Primarily funded with property and sales tax revenues and pays for services like police protection, residential garbage collection and building and maintenance of recreation facilities. Stormwater and water and sewer funds — Enterprise funds that rely on user fees to pay for their specific services. No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. Strategic Plan The town uses a strategic plan to align available resources with priorities. The plan is used in coordination with the budget and the Comprehensive Sustainability Plan. Name: 13_county_orange_shelters_shelter
Representative_Docs: ['Emergency shelters in Orange County are provided by Orange County,\xa0Inter-Faith Council for Social Service and\xa0the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross.', "Shelters Emergency shelters in Orange County are provided by Orange County,\xa0Inter-Faith Council for Social Service and\xa0the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross. Cold Weather Shelters Cold Weather Cots is an emergency shelter program run jointly by Orange County and the Inter-Faith Council for Social Service. The program provides shelter for residents living unhoused during the winter. Inter-Faith Council for Social Service The IFC shelter locations are open every night November through March. Orange County The Orange County shelter location is open on nights when the temperature is at or below 32 degrees. People needing transportation to the county shelter may contact the county's\xa0street outreach team at 919-886-3351 by 6 p.m. each day for assistance. Men, women and families are welcome. Walk-ins are accepted. Cots, blankets and snacks are provided.\xa0Call 919-245-2490 to hear more information. Emergency Shelters Orange County officials work with the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross to open emergency shelters in two primary locations: Orange Middle School, 308 Orange High School Road, Hillsborough Smith Middle School, 9201 Seawell School Road, Chapel Hill Shelter Reminders Before and During Shelter Openings Shelters require residents to be as informed and prepared as possible. If you will need special assistance, call 919-245-2884 to register for assistance before a disaster strikes. For emergency transportation, call 919-245-6100. Shelter locations may change depending on the emergency. Listen to the radio for directions concerning shelter openings. Shelters will have food and water but will not have easy access to medications or other personal needs. Smoking is not allowed in shelters. Bring Photo identification Regularly taken medications Insurance information Bedding Towels Comfortable clothing Personal radios with headphones Games, reading material or other things to pass the time — especially for children Specialty items for your personal needs Do Not Bring Weapons Illegal drugs Candles Valuables Pets, Service Animals and Livestock Form a plan for your pets to keep them safe during severe weather. See Prepare Your Pets for Disasters on Ready.gov. Orange County has the following regulations for animals at shelters: Service animals for the hearing and visually impaired are allowed in shelters. Orange County Animal Services staff will be on hand to board pets in the best manner possible. Owners of large animals and livestock should make preparations for their animals. Very limited support for these animals will be provided in a disaster.", "Emergency shelters in Orange County are provided by Orange County,\xa0Inter-Faith Council for Social Service and\xa0the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross. People needing transportation to the county shelter may contact the county's\xa0street outreach team at 919-886-3351 by 6 p.m. each day for assistance. Men, women and families are welcome. Walk-ins are accepted. Cots, blankets and snacks are provided.\xa0Call 919-245-2490 to hear more information. Emergency Shelters Orange County officials work with the Orange County Chapter of the American Red Cross to open emergency shelters in two primary locations: Orange Middle School, 308 Orange High School Road, Hillsborough Smith Middle School, 9201 Seawell School Road, Chapel Hill Shelter Reminders Before and During Shelter Openings Shelters require residents to be as informed and prepared as possible. If you will need special assistance, call 919-245-2884 to register for assistance before a disaster strikes. For emergency transportation, call 919-245-6100. Shelter locations may change depending on the emergency. Listen to the radio for directions concerning shelter openings. Shelters will have food and water but will not have easy access to medications or other personal needs. Smoking is not allowed in shelters. Bring Photo identification Regularly taken medications Insurance information Bedding Towels Comfortable clothing Personal radios with headphones Games, reading material or other things to pass the time — especially for children Specialty items for your personal needs Do Not Bring Weapons Illegal drugs Candles Valuables Pets, Service Animals and Livestock Form a plan for your pets to keep them safe during severe weather. See Prepare Your Pets for Disasters on Ready.gov. Orange County has the following regulations for animals at shelters: Service animals for the hearing and visually impaired are allowed in shelters. Orange County Animal Services staff will be on hand to board pets in the best manner possible. Owners of large animals and livestock should make preparations for their animals. Very limited support for these animals will be provided in a disaster."]
