Name: 15_treatment_quality_wastewater_testing
Representative_Docs: ["Annual Reports Hillsborough's treatment plants regularly test for contaminants and prepare annual reports about their treatment processes and testing. Water Quality Report An annual report is published in July\xa0for the previous year. It includes  information on our water treatment system and performance as well as water quality information from the Environmental Protection Agency. Water Quality Report Wastewater Quality Report A report is published in August each year for the previous fiscal year (July 1-June 30). It includes information on our wastewater collection and treatment systems and their performance. Wastewater Quality Report Distribution and Collection Division The Water Distribution and Wastewater Collection Division: Repairs water line leaks. Cleans and repairs sewer lines. Maintains water tanks, booster pump stations and sewage lift stations. Staff members are responsible for programs and projects to maintain and protect the utility system. See: About Water About Wastewater Treatment Plants The town’s treatment plants prepare drinking water and clean used water. They regularly test for contaminants. Water Treatment Plant The water plant takes water from the Eno River, cleans and disinfects it, and then pumps it to customers. The state requires that the plant change its disinfection method for one month each year to clean the distribution system. About Water Wastewater Treatment Plant The wastewater plant removes waste and nutrients from used water, disinfects the water with chlorine, and then removes the chlorine before releasing the water into the Eno River. About Wastewater", "Our Water Quality The town's water treatment must meet high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than 150 substances in the town's drinking water and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Water Quality Report . Water Plant Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has: A state-certified laboratory to test for bacteria daily. Two lab-certified operators who test raw and finished water at the plant every two hours\xa0— more than required by the state. Water System Testing Staff also test various points in the water system. The water system includes the pipes, pumps and tanks needed to transport and store water. Tests occur: Weekly\xa0— For bacteria at five of the farthest points in the town's water lines. Monthly — For bacteria at 15 areas. Quarterly — For disinfection byproducts. These are substances created by the process to clean water. Annually — For inorganic materials, pesticides, synthetic organic chemicals, nitrates, volatile organic compounds and secondary contaminants. Every five years — For unregulated contaminants that the Environmental Protection Agency lists for testing. Our Water Treatment Process The Hillsborough Water Treatment Plant is the processing plant for your water. The plant takes raw water from the Eno River. It then adds ingredients and applies processes to the water to make it usable. The plant focuses on: First\xa0— Cleanliness and compliance with standards. Second\xa0— Taste, odor and appearance. Water Treatment Converting raw water into drinking water includes injecting chemicals at multiple points to help remove organic\xa0material, disinfect the water and ensure longer freshness before releasing it for use by the community. The\xa0chemical process starts before the raw water reaches the Water Treatment Plant. Water released from the town's reservoir travels to Lake Ben Johnston, where the town removes water for use.\xa0At this intake location, a chemical is added to help particles in the water combine for easier removal. At the plant, more\xa0chemicals are added for the same reason and to adjust the water's color,\xa0acidity and alkalinity. Filtering Out Materials The water then goes through several filtration, aeration and clarification stages. In these stages, the water remains in large basins while being mixed at slower and slower speeds. This allows the combined large materials to drop to the bottom of the basins. Finally, the clear water\xa0moves through a fine filtration system to remove remaining particles.\xa0At this stage, the water’s turbidity is measured and filtration\xa0adjustments are made to remove any cloudiness from the water caused by large amounts of particles that\xa0are otherwise invisible to the eye. Adjusting Treatment The water is then disinfected and final adjustments to acidity and\xa0alkalinity are made before the water is pumped through pipes to homes and businesses. Throughout the\xa0treatment process, employees test the water for more than 150 contaminants, making adjustments to the\xa0treatment as needed. See: From River to Faucet: Hillsborough's Water Treatment Process video Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Request a Tour", "Our Treated Wastewater Quality The town's wastewater treatment must meet extremely high standards set by the town, state and federal governments.\xa0Hillsborough routinely tests for more than\xa0220\xa0substances in the town's wastewater and solids and meets or exceeds the standards.\xa0See the annual Wastewater Quality Report . Testing Staff monitor and evaluate water 24 hours a day.\xa0The plant has a\xa0laboratory to perform frequent checks on the progress of wastewater treatment. Staff also test various points in the Eno River.  Tests occur: Upstream of the water intake Before and after wastewater discharge Downstream of wastewater discharge Our Wastewater Treatment Process The plant uses an enhanced biological nutrient removal process to treat wastewater.\xa0Naturally occurring nutrients can cause algae growth, depleted oxygen, and fish kills. The nutrients\xa0— like nitrogen and\xa0phosphorus — must be\xa0brought to safe levels before treated wastewater can be discharged into the Eno River. Treatment Steps The Town of Hillsborough's wastewater treatment plant Large item removal — Bar screens remove trash, wood and rocks, which then are compacted for disposal. Grit removal — Sand and dirt are removed for disposal. Secondary treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are removed through a series of aeration tanks and sent to large tanks called digesters (see 4). The remaining wastewater moves to the clarifiers (see 5). Solids treatment — Organic materials and nutrients are treated in digesters and then thickened and trucked to a composting facility to be turned into a soil amendment. Clarification — Rakes slowly skim the wastewater surface for scum like grease, oils, plastics and soap. The scum is pumped to digesters (see 4). Solids settle and are removed for disposal. Sand filtration — The remaining water moves through 6 feet of sand, which removes additional solids. Disinfection, final treatment — Chlorine is added to kill bacteria. Chemicals are added to neutralize the chlorine. Aeration — The water is re-aerated before discharge to remove dissolved gases and to oxidize dissolved metals. Discharge — The treated wastewater is returned to the Eno River at an outfall. Wastewater Treatment Plant and Tours Wastewater Treatment Plant employees are highly certified and operate and maintain the plant to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of wastewater per day.\xa0It was built in 1977, with upgrades in 2001 and\xa02007 to improve solids management, nutrient removal and\xa0treatment capabilities. The most recent expansion and upgrade was completed in 2014, allowing the plant to meet state\xa0requirements to protect\xa0Falls Lake, where Hillsborough’s watershed drains. This upgrade Hillsborough’s discharged water\xa0quality among the best in the nation. Awards In previous years, the Wastewater Treatment Plant and its staff have won awards for: Professionalism in the Operation of a Wastewater Treatment Plant Solutions to Operational Problems Wastewater Treatment Plant Operations and Maintenance Wastewater Treatment Plant Operator Tours See what it takes to treat used water for return to the Eno River. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require a completed request form. Request a Tour"]

Name: 16_scam_email_permit_permits
Representative_Docs: ["Scam Alert An email scam is posing as the Town of Hillsborough and requests payment for permit fees. Read More The town has requirements for operating a business in town limits. Nonprofit organizations and merchants selling prepared food or beverages in temporary locations as part of special events must follow requirements for mobile food vendors. Businesses  may need additional permits and must comply with town taxes for prepared food and beverages and hotel and motel room occupancy. Historic District Permits Zoning Compliance Permits Economic Development Pay Online Pay by Other Methods Tax Rates and Compliance Fees and Charges Schedule Permits and Licenses BUSINESS LICENSE All businesses selling beer or\xa0wine must obtain a business license. No other type of business is required to obtain a license. Apply for Business License Contact Financial Services OUTDOOR SEATING PERMIT An outdoor seating permit allows businesses to serve customers at tables outside on the public sidewalk and in public space. These conditions apply: Tables must be placed directly next to the business’s storefront. Tables must belong to the business. Seating areas must be clearly delineated. A 5-foot path must be available that leads to the business's front door and that complies with the Americans with Disabilities Act. A 6-foot clear pedestrian path must be maintained along sidewalks. Application and Materials Sign Indemnity Agreement Apply for Outdoor Seating Permit See more information: Examples of Sidewalk Dining Areas and Materials Outdoor Seating and Merchandising Area Diagrams Contact Economic Development Mobile Food Vendors Mobile Food Vendor Regulations Mobile food vendors must have operating permits from the town and the Orange County Health Department . Vendors include pushcart and food truck operators. Regulations apply to those\xa0who intend to operate from a fixed location or through a route multiple days per year. See Mobile Food Vendors in the Code of Ordinances. Regular Operations within Town The town permits a specific\xa0number of food trucks within its limits for more permanent operations. Permits are issued on a first-come, first-served basis until all available permits have been issued. Apply for Permit Monthly and Single-Day Tax Compliance Mobile food vendors are required to make food and beverage tax payments: Monthly Compliance —\xa0Vendors working in a more permanent basis must file a report each month regardless of sales. Single-Day Compliance —\xa0Nonprofit organizations and merchants selling prepared food or beverages in temporary locations must submit an application and prepay for each day of operation. Tax Rates and Compliance Additional Requirements Fats, Oil and Grease Properties that discharge greasy wastewater are required to have interceptors for fats, oil and grease to keep these materials out of plumbing and sewer lines. Over time, this waste can cause sewer backups or overflows. Fats, Oil and Grease Policy Proper Disposal of Fats, Oils and Grease Protect the Sewer System Contact Utilities Outdoor Merchandising Retailers may place merchandise outside without a permit. For requirements, see: Economic Development page Streets and Sidewalks in the Code of Ordinances Staff Title Phone Email McCole, Dave Finance Director 919-296-9451 Email Campbell, Shannan Planning and Economic Development Manager 919-296-9477 Email Miller, Troy Utilities Infrastructure Protection Supervisor 919-296-9653 Email", 'Scam Alert An email scam is posing as the Town of Hillsborough and requests payment for permit fees. Read More The Planning and Economic Development Division issues zoning compliance permits for activities regulated under the\xa0Unified Development Ordinance that are requested on property in the town\'s limits and planning area. In some cases, a building permit may be required. The Orange County Planning and Inspections Department oversees building permits and inspections. Unified Development Ordinance Pay Online Pay by Other Methods Change of Use Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued for changes of use, such as moving a business into an existing non-residential retail or office space. Apply for Change of Use Permit Home Occupation Permit Before starting a home business, contact planning staff to describe the business you plan to open. Staff will help determine if the type of business you want to open meets conditions for a zoning compliance permit. The regulations guide and checklist below lists businesses prohibited from home occupation permits. Home Occupation FAQs Starting a Home Occupation Local Regulations Guide and Checklist Apply for Home Occupation Permit New Construction and Additions Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued for site changes, such as relocating or expanding an existing structure or constructing a new building, accessory building, deck or fence. See a sample plot plan . Apply for Permit Signs Permit Zoning compliance permits are issued before installing new signs or changing the location, sign face or message of an existing sign. Apply for Signs Permit Additional Information Accessory Dwelling Units Accessory dwelling units are secondary dwellings added to the property of a main dwelling. A dwelling unit must contain cooking, sleeping and bathing facilities. A kitchen must have cooking equipment and a sink. A bathroom must have a sink, toilet and shower or bathtub. Some neighborhoods have covenants that restrict multiple units. Property owners are responsible for checking with their homeowners association before applying for permits. Types of Accessory Units The town has defined two types of accessory units. Freestanding Units These include garage apartments or "granny flats." The parcel must be zoned Residential 10, 15 or 20 (R-10, R-15 or R-20). These designations are common to neighborhoods built before the town implemented special use zoning around the year 2000. Neighborhoods in special use zoning districts are not able to build freestanding units. They are built to higher densities and often don’t have lots large enough to accommodate a freestanding unit. These neighborhoods include Collins Ridge, Fiori Hill, Forest Ridge and Waterstone Estates. In-home Units These are fully attached to the main dwelling, such as an efficiency or basement apartment. The accessory unit must have its own exterior access. If interior access to the principal dwelling is provided, it must be lockable from both dwellings. Difference Between Zoning and Building Permits Building permits deal with compliance with the state building code for structural, electrical, mechanical and plumbing work. Orange County issues these permits and enforces these codes. The Town of Hillsborough issues zoning permits to verify that the use of a structure is allowed in a given district and that structures are not too tall or too close to the property line. Any project that changes the footprint of a structure or adds a new structure or fence requires a zoning permit if the lot is within the town’s planning jurisdiction. Interactive Zoning Map For detailed zoning information, use the town\'s interactive zoning map. See the guide for instructions. Use Interactive Zoning Map Submitting an Application Applications can be submitted: By email to planning@hillsboroughnc.gov. In person at Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. By mail to Planning and Economic Development Division, PO Box 429, Hillsborough, NC 27278. Staff typically process permit applications in one to two business days. Contact Information Town Hall, 101 E. Orange St. 919-296-9470 Please make an appointment for in-person visits. Contact Planning Staff Title Phone Email Boyle, Molly Senior Planner 919-296-9473 Email Kimbrough, Dakotah Planning Technician 919-296-9470 Email', 'Subscribe to news releases or sign up for emergency alerts . Scam Alert: New Email Scam Poses as Town Asking for Fees Take precautions to avoid scams Post Date: August 11, 2025 4:00 p.m. A new email scam poses as the Town of Hillsborough and asks for payment. Updated Nov. 6: This version includes a link to the FBI\'s Internet Crime Complaint Center for reporting the scam email. The Town of Hillsborough is aware of a new email scam that requests payment for permit fees. You can avoid being scammed by: Checking with the town or other government agency that is supposedly asking you to pay for a permit or other fees. Not replying to the email, which often requests that you reply to receive instructions on how to pay. The new scam appears to be sent from an email that ends in “plannings.us” or “hillsboroughnc.gov@usa.com" and may reference a real, past or present home improvement project at your address. The email asks for fees on behalf of the town’s planning and economic development division. Town emails end in “hillsboroughnc.gov.” News releases and bid postings sent by email come from townofhillsborough@public.govdelivery.com. If you receive an email that seems to be from the Town of Hillsborough and you would like to check on its validity, contact a town employee at an email address or phone number that you have previously used or through the town’s Contact Us page. Or call the town’s general number at 919-732-1270. Four Signs It’s a Scam The Federal Trade Commission notes scammers typically: Pretend to be from an organization you know, even showing up on your phone with a spoofed caller identification. Say there’s a problem with your account or a prize. Pressure you to act immediately. Tell you to pay in a specific way. Report Scams The Hillsborough Police Department asks those who receive this scam email to report it to the FBI’s Internet Crime Complaint Center . Note that there is a scam alert on the center’s website about scammers impersonating the Internet Crime Complaint Center. The center will not directly contact someone who files a complaint asking for information or money. In addition to reporting the scam email to the FBI, it is advised that you follow this advice from the Federal Trade Commission: Report the scam to the organization or company that was imitated by calling a number you know is real. Report the scam to the commission at ReportFraud.ftc.gov . Report the scam to your state attorney general . Let other people know about the scam so they can avoid it, too. If you have paid a scammer, see the Federal Trade Commission’s What to Do If You Were Scammed page, which includes information on how you might get your money back. Return to full list >> Subscribe to News Subscribe to Emergency Alerts']

Representative_Docs: ['Awareness Property owners with utilities easements need to be aware of the easement boundaries before building or planting. Contact the Engineering Services Division to: Learn if you have an easement on your property. Request permission before placing anything in an easement. Upkeep and Clearances Property owners are responsible for upkeep, such as mowing, of their property where easements are located and within the road right of way. Property owners also must maintain sufficient clearance to allow the town to access, operate and maintain its assets. C learance requirements are measured from the center point (radius) of the device: Fire hydrants\xa0— 3 feet Manholes and concrete vaults\xa0—\xa06 feet Water meters, valve boxes, sewer cleanouts and other utilities features not listed\xa0—\xa02 feet Additional information on the technical specifications for utilities and sanitary sewer use can be found in the Hillsborough Code of Ordinances , chapters 14 and 15.', 'Customer Responsibilities Awareness Property owners with utilities easements need to be aware of the easement boundaries before building or planting. Contact the Engineering Services Division to: Learn if you have an easement on your property. Request permission before placing anything in an easement. Upkeep and Clearances Property owners are responsible for upkeep, such as mowing, of their property where easements are located and within the road right of way. Property owners also must maintain sufficient clearance to allow the town to access, operate and maintain its assets. C learance requirements are measured from the center point (radius) of the device: Fire hydrants\xa0— 3 feet Manholes and concrete vaults\xa0—\xa06 feet Water meters, valve boxes, sewer cleanouts and other utilities features not listed\xa0—\xa02 feet Additional information on the technical specifications for utilities and sanitary sewer use can be found in the Hillsborough Code of Ordinances , chapters 14 and 15.', "A right of way or easement gives legal right for public uses or to perform work on infrastructure. They are documented to reserve property for the specific use, such as maintaining the town's water and sewer system. Easements do not remove property from owners, but they must be kept clear of any plantings or structures. Town Responsibilities The town periodically mows water and sewer easements to keep them accessible for routine maintenance and emergency response related to town utility assets. The mowing typically occurs in the spring and fall and includes the removal of brush and undergrowth. The town will not replace any plants, fences, sheds or other structures that are removed from a utilities easement when utilities workers need to access the easement. The town is responsible for any repairs to property damaged by heavy equipment used in the clearing. Name: 19_line_property_sewer_meter
Representative_Docs: ["The responsibility for repairs of water lines depends on whether the water line is on the utility or the property owner's side of the water meter or service connection. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the water main. The supply system's main line typically is located under roadways. The town also maintains water service connections in public rights of ways or easements. It will make repairs to the line in that area. Property owners are responsible for the water service line between their property's house or structure and the water meter or service connection. Water Meter A property's water meter typically indicates the ownership line. The meter is placed on the line between the property's edge and the public right of way or easement. If a water meter is not located in the public right of way or easement,\xa0the town will provide water service maintenance to the meter and may require relocation during redevelopment.", "The town\xa0assesses sewer blockages to determine the cause and location — whether they are\xa0on the utility or property owner's side\xa0of a\xa0sewer line. Customers may be responsible for the cost of investigation and repairs if their\xa0 actions cause a blockage in the town’s sewer main. Responsibilities The town owns and maintains the sewer main or sewer service line in public rights of ways or easements. It will clear blockages of lines in public rights of ways or easements. The town may require property owners to install or relocate cleanouts to the town's specifications. The town may agree to perform the work but may require reimbursement and the granting of property access from the customer. Property owners are responsible for blockages in the sewer service line between the house or structure on their property and the public right of way or easement.\xa0The town can require installation or relocation of a cleanout at the right of way or easement if one is not present. Sewer Cleanout A sewer cleanout is a capped pipe that allows access to the sewer line for maintenance and inspections. Cleanouts typically indicate the ownership line when located at the\xa0edge of the public right of way or easement.", "Blocked Sewer Lines The town\xa0assesses sewer blockages to determine the cause and location — whether they are\xa0on the utility or property owner's side\xa0of a\xa0sewer line. Customers may be responsible for the cost of investigation and repairs if their\xa0 actions cause a blockage in the town’s sewer main. The town may agree to perform the work but may require reimbursement and the granting of property access from the customer. Property owners are responsible for blockages in the sewer service line between the house or structure on their property and the public right of way or easement.\xa0The town can require installation or relocation of a cleanout at the right of way or easement if one is not present. Name: 20_stormwater_runoff_properties_measures
Representative_Docs: ["Stormwater utility fees fund compliance with state and federal stormwater regulations.\xa0The primary purpose for stormwater management in Hillsborough is to\xa0reduce or eliminate stormwater runoff pollution from reaching local waterbodies, including the Eno River, which is the town's source for drinking water. Stormwater runoff pollution: Hurts aquatic ecosystems. Poses human health risks. Can greatly increase the cost of treating water for drinking. Services for community members that are funded by the fees include: Assistance with drainage and stormwater runoff concerns. Educational programs for schools and civic groups. Maintenance of stormwater drainage within town-owned streets and facilities. Review of new development proposals to ensure compliance with stormwater requirements. Stream buffer program to protect streams. Maintenance of stormwater drainage within town-owned streets and facilities. Review of new development proposals to ensure compliance with stormwater requirements. Stream buffer program to protect streams. See Stormwater Services .", "Assistance or Discounts No assistance or discounts are available at this time. Because stormwater runoff is generated from all properties, the fee is assessed to all properties regardless of tax status or profitability. Town staff are considering a: Voluntary Assistance Program\xa0— This would be similar to the program in place for water and sewer bills. See the Water Assistance Program . Discount for Special Measures — This would be for non-residential properties that install engineered stormwater control measures exceeding the requirements for new development to protect water quality. These control measures also are known as stormwater best management practices. Stormwater Fees Purpose Stormwater utility fees fund compliance with state and federal stormwater regulations.\xa0The primary purpose for stormwater management in Hillsborough is to\xa0reduce or eliminate stormwater runoff pollution from reaching local waterbodies, including the Eno River, which is the town's source for drinking water. Stormwater runoff pollution: Hurts aquatic ecosystems. Poses human health risks. Can greatly increase the cost of treating water for drinking. Services for community members that are funded by the fees include: Assistance with drainage and stormwater runoff concerns. Educational programs for schools and civic groups. Maintenance of stormwater drainage within town-owned streets and facilities. Review of new development proposals to ensure compliance with stormwater requirements. Stream buffer program to protect streams. See Stormwater Services . Understanding Enterprise Funds The Stormwater Utility Fund is one of two enterprise funds operated by the town. The other is the Water and Sewer Fund. Enterprise funds account for the business-like activities of the government. These services are funded with revenues generated from fees and charges rather than taxes. No profits are made in enterprise funds, and the revenue is used for no other purpose. The Hillsborough Stormwater Fund pays for stormwater services. The revenue for the fund comes\xa0primarily from stormwater fees. See the Stormwater Management Utility Ordinance ."]

Name: 21_waste_composting_sites_food
Representative_Docs: ['Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Drop-off Sites Food waste can be dropped off for free at 11 sites in Orange County, including: Eno River Farmers Market in downtown Hillsborough. Orange County 24-hour recycling drop-off site in Hampton Pointe. Orange County waste and recycling center on Walnut Grove Church Road, north of Hillsborough. The North Carolina Department of Environmental Quality adds public and private composting sites to the NC Food Donation and Compost Resource Map as they become available. Acceptable Items Items that can be dropped off for composting at these sites includes: Food scraps. Food-related paper, including napkins, greasy pizza boxes and staple-free tea bags. Flowers and trimmings from small indoor plants. For more information on the drop-off sites and a comprehensive list of acceptable items, see the Orange County food waste page. Composting at Home Composting can be done at home and can reduce fertilizer, herbicide and water needs for plants. Learn how to get started on the Orange County Cooperative Extension website . For those without the space, conditions or desire for backyard composting, countertop composters can be purchased to provide a soil additive with less effort. Countertop composters may use a combination of grinders and heat to reduce food volume and create compost.', 'Food Waste Composting food waste reduces disposal fees the town must pay and benefits the environment. Fees and Violations Fees can be paid several ways.\xa0Contact the administrative support specialist for more information. Cash or check at the Town Hall Annex, 105 E. Corbin St. Credit or debit card through the town's payments and donations website . Violations If requirements for collection are not followed, the garbage or bulk items will not be collected. An employee will leave information on the town's policy for the resident. If trash carts and uncollected garbage are not removed from the street as required, a notice of violation will be delivered to the resident by mail or by town staff.\xa0A fee of $25 per day will be assessed if the resident does not comply with the ordinance within five days of the notice date. Recycling and Other Solid Waste Services Orange County collects recycling at curbs on Wednesdays in Hillsborough. See the county's Urban and Town Curbside (Weekly Collection) page for requirements. The county also provides other trash services to Hillsborough residents.\xa0Contact Orange County Solid Waste Management for these services, including: Recycling drop-off sites. Household hazardous waste collection. Construction and demolition waste collection. Solid waste convenience centers. Rollout Carts For collection and safety, carts must be: Placed at the curb between 4 p.m. the day before collection and 6:30 a.m. the day of collection. Placed within 3 feet of the curb, with the handle facing away from the street. Placed at least 4 feet from parked cars, trees or other obstructions. Removed from the street by 6 a.m. the day after collection. See our video Position Your Solid Waste and Recycling Carts Properly . Rollout Cart Delivery and Repair Residents in single-family homes with active utility accounts may receive one free trash rollout cart after account activation if they live in town limits on a publicly owned road. See the Services for Your Property page to determine whether a property is in town limits. A fee is charged for a second cart. Payment must be received before delivery is scheduled. See the fees section for how to pay the fee. Place a work order or call the administrative support specialist to request: Cart delivery or repair Second cart"]

Name: 22_roads_streets_maintained_street
Representative_Docs: ['The Hillsborough Public Works Division oversees maintenance of town-owned streets. Some roads in town are private or maintained by the state. See the Street Maintenance page to learn more.', 'Street Repairs and Repaving Report potholes and other damage to town-maintained streets through the Public Works contact form or by calling the administrative support specialist. Provide the specific location of the problem, such as the nearest street address or intersection. Call 911 to report conditions that pose an immediate threat to safety. Repaving Hillsborough repaves streets about every 20 years. Annually, it tries to repave about 5% of its roads with town and state funds. Staff pick streets based on their condition and age.\xa0About every five years, the town pays an engineering firm to analyze the condition of town-maintained roads. Some roads may be sealed or patched to extend their use. Name: 24_theft_box_legally_obtain
Representative_Docs: ['Theft from Hydrants If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the utility system superintendent. To help the town prosecute the case: Discreetly take a photo if possible. Note the time, date, location, vehicle name and number, and license plate. Legally Obtained Water Contractors are able to purchase bulk water from the town. They can: Obtain water from the Water Treatment Plant by paying in advance at the Town Hall Annex. Rent water meters to legally obtain water from a fire hydrant. The meters\xa0are housed in an orange box with a backflow prevention device to safely obtain water. See the Start or Stop Service page.', "Customers fund the operation of the town's water and sewer system, including the infrastructure needed to treat, transport and protect water. Please immediately call 911 to report any tampering or theft. See Fees and Charges Schedule for fees and penalties. See Utilities: Prohibited Activities in the Code of Ordinances. Water can be legally and safely obtained from a fire hydrant with an orange box. Billing and Adjustments Start or Stop Service Tampering and Theft of Infrastructure Backflow Prevention Devices The town own some backflow prevention devices on town properties. Most are privately owned. If you see anyone tampering with a backflow prevention device or have experienced a theft of the device: Call 911 to have an officer dispatched. Contact the utilities infrastructure protection supervisor. The devices are housed in utility boxes and prevent contaminated water from entering potable water in a building. They can be targets for theft due to their metal content. Property owners who have had a backflow preventer stolen or damaged will need to employ the services of a plumber. Preventing Theft Options for preventing theft include: Employing community or neighborhood watch prevention, asking community members to report any suspicious behavior to police. Adding a lock to the backflow preventer box or installing a locking box, available from a plumber or from a plumbing warehouse. If locks are added, access must be given to the town. Cutting back any shrubs or other plantings to allow some view of the device and ensuring that an area of at least 5 feet around the utility is free of any growth. Water Meters Any tampering of a meter or service connection will result in the meter being locked and a tampering fee and civil penalty. Service will not be reconnected until all fees are paid in full. Repeated tampering will result in removal of the meter, additional fees to reset and reactivate it, and potential civil or criminal charges. Theft of Water Safety and Operational Concerns Water theft can cause concerns for the public water system including: Water hammer (form of hydraulic shock) in the system that breaks pipes and damages equipment. Lowered water pressure for customers during the time of theft. Contamination of the public water system. Costs associated with repairing damage to the system. Theft from Hydrants If you see a person or business taking water from a water hydrant without an orange meter box, report it immediately to 911 and to the utility system superintendent. Contact Information Adron F. Thompson Facility, 715 Dimmocks Mill Road 919-296-9650 Office Hours — 8 a.m. to 5 p.m. Monday-Friday. Emergencies Tampering or Theft —\xa0Call 911 and then use the contact utilities form. Water and Sewer Emergencies — Report immediately by calling 919-732-3621. Name: 26_business_checklist_economic_starting
Representative_Docs: ["Below are steps that must be taken when starting, expanding or relocating a business. See the checklist for detailed information. Determine Zoning District ― Get help from economic development staff in determining the zoning district most appropriate for your\xa0business type\xa0before you sign a lease, purchase a building or enter into a land\xa0agreement. Select Possible Sites ― Economic development staff can suggest possible sites for your business. The town works\xa0closely with the Orange County Economic Development Office to keep\xa0an updated inventory of available sites. Meet about Development and Occupancy ―\xa0Hillsborough economic development staff will assist you in setting up a meeting with town and Orange County staff to discuss property development, new construction or the upfit of space to be leased. The town contracts with the county for building inspection services. Items to be discussed include: Compliance with the\xa0Americans with Disabilities Act, building codes and\xa0Health Department requirements for restaurants and food service\xa0establishments. Requirements for\xa0parking, lighting, landscaping, grading, grease traps, backflow\xa0prevention, fire access and fire suppression. Seek Permitting and Inspections ― Apply for all the\xa0permits necessary to start work on a space or to occupy the space once you've\xa0selected a site or existing building.\xa0These permits will have been discussed more extensively in the\xa0predevelopment meeting. Close the Project ― Economic development staff offer an optional meeting with business owners and developers at a project's conclusion to\xa0identify issues or challenges that may have impacted the project’s budget or timeline. The\xa0confidential meetings help improve the town's development processes. Name: 27_adjustment_leak_leaks_adjustments
Representative_Docs: ['Leak Adjustment Customers may request a bill adjustment if a water line break has resulted in significantly greater charges for a billing period. One of the following is required: A plumber or maintenance person must provide sufficient documentation of a leak. Customers must provide receipts for items purchased for repairs. Customers must request leak adjustments no later than two months after the billing date for the adjustment. Customers are allowed one leak adjustment in a 12-month period. If an adjustment is approved, the customer will be charged an amount based on an average of the previous six months. Leak Adjustment Request Leak Adjustment Request (Español) Malfunctioning Water Meter If a meter fails to register water use, the customer will be charged an amount for water and sewer usage based on an average of the previous six months. The standard for meter accuracy is plus or minus 10%. A meter flow test can be requested, with a fee charged if the meter passes the flow test. Name: 29_tours_plant_treatment_operator
Representative_Docs: ['Water Treatment Plant and Tours The Water Treatment Plant employees are highly certified. They continually operate and maintain the plant 24 hours a day to ensure compliance with state and federal permits and regulations. The plant can treat up to 3 million gallons of drinking water per day. Awards In previous years, the Water Treatment Plant and its staff won an award for Outstanding Operator of the Year from the N.C. Waterworks Operators Association. Tours See what it takes to prepare water taken from the Eno River for drinking. Public tours are offered at 1 p.m. on the second Friday of the month in March and September. Private tours can be scheduled separately for special groups. All tours are open to a maximum of 10 people with participants at least 10 years old. All tours require complete a request form. Name: 31_stop_service_online_start
Representative_Docs: ["Start Service Contact customer service at least three business days in advance of the requested start date. A $20 fee is charged to transfer accounts between addresses. To start service, the town requires all of the following: Connection fee of $20 (non-refundable) Deposit of $75 in town or $150 out of town Copy of one of the following signed by all parties: Deed, settlement statement or purchase agreement Lease agreement Property management agreement Social Security number Valid driver’s license, government ID or passport Starting Service Create an account on the town's online payment site to start water service. Learn more about the online payments site on the Billing and Adjustments page. Start Service Stop Service Submit a request to disconnect service at least three business days before the requested end date. Stop Service Sign in to the town's online payments site or sign up for an account to stop water service.\xa0Learn more about the online payments site on the Billing and Adjustments page. Stop Service", "Hillsborough provides water and sewer services within town limits and to some properties outside town limits. Connection —\xa0Service should be requested at least three business days in advance. See the Start or Stop Service page. Electronic Bills and Online Payment —\xa0Water and sewer customers are encouraged to sign up for electronic bills and to pay online through the payments and donations site .
